This verification runs in time polynomial in $n$, satisfying the definition of NP. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
**State the formal definition of the complexity class P. Give two example problems that belong to P and briefly explain why.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A language $L$ is in **P** if there exists a deterministic Turing Machine $M$ and a polynomial $p$ such that for every input $x$:
1. $x \in L \iff M$ accepts $x$, and
2. $M$ halts within $p(\vert x \vert)$ steps on input $x$.

Equivalently, $P = \bigcup_{k \geq 0} DTIME(n^k)$. **(1 mark)**

**Example 1 — Sorting (decision version):** Given a list of $n$ numbers and a proposed ordering, decide whether the list is sorted in non-decreasing order. This can be checked in $O(n)$ time by a single linear scan, which is polynomial. **(1 mark)**

**Example 2 — Graph Reachability (PATH):** Given a directed graph $G$ and two vertices $s, t$, decide whether $t$ is reachable from $s$. Breadth-First Search solves this in $O(n + m) = O(n^2)$ time on a graph with $n$ vertices and $m$ edges, which is polynomial in the input size. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). You may be given an internal choice between two questions; both are provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Adapted from Dec 2022]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define the complexity class NP using both the nondeterministic Turing Machine and the verifier characterizations. Show that these two definitions are equivalent.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Prove that the problem **CLIQUE** ∈ NP, where CLIQUE asks: given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $k$, does $G$ contain a complete subgraph on $k$ vertices (a $k$-clique)?

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Two Definitions and Their Equivalence**

**Definition 1 (Nondeterministic TM view):** $L \in NP$ if there exists a nondeterministic Turing Machine $N$ and a polynomial $p$ such that for every input $x$, every computation branch of $N$ halts within $p(\vert x \vert)$ steps, and $x \in L \iff$ at least one branch of $N$ accepts. **(2 marks)**

**Definition 2 (Verifier view):** $L \in NP$ if there exists a polynomial-time deterministic algorithm $V$ and a polynomial $p$ such that:
$$x \in L \iff \exists y \text{ with } \vert y \vert \leq p(\vert x \vert) \text{ and } V(x, y) = \text{ACCEPT}$$
**(2 marks)**

**Equivalence Proof Sketch:**

*Direction 1: NTM → Verifier.* Suppose $L$ is decided by an NTM $N$ in time $p(n)$. For any $x \in L$, there exists an accepting branch. Encode the sequence of nondeterministic choices made along that branch as the certificate $y$; this sequence has length at most $p(\vert x \vert)$ (one choice per step). The verifier $V$ simulates $N$ on $x$, using $y$ to resolve nondeterministic choices deterministically. If the simulated branch accepts, $V$ accepts. The simulation runs in $O(p(n)^2) = \text{poly}(n)$ time. **(1.5 marks)**

*Direction 2: Verifier → NTM.* Suppose $L$ has a polynomial-time verifier $V$ with certificate bound $p(n)$. Construct an NTM $N$ that, on input $x$, first nondeterministically writes a string $y$ of length $p(\vert x \vert)$ on a separate tape, then simulates $V(x, y)$. If $V$ accepts, $N$ accepts. The certificate length is bounded by $p(\vert x \vert)$, and the simulation of $V$ adds only polynomial overhead, so $N$ runs in polynomial time. **(1.5 marks)**

Since the two definitions accept exactly the same set of languages, they are equivalent. $\blacksquare$

**(b) — Proof that CLIQUE ∈ NP**

**Problem Restatement:** Given graph $G = (V, E)$ with $\vert V \vert = n$ vertices and an integer $k$ (where $1 \leq k \leq n$), determine whether $G$ contains a clique of size $k$ (a subset $S \subseteq V$ with $\vert S \vert = k$ such that every pair of vertices in $S$ is connected by an edge). **(1 mark)**

**Certificate:** Let the certificate $y$ be the subset $S \subseteq V$ claimed to be a $k$-clique. Encode $S$ as a bit string of length $n$ where bit $i$ is 1 iff vertex $v_i \in S$. Thus $\vert y \vert = n$, which is polynomial in the input length. **(1.5 marks)**

**Verifier Algorithm $V(x, y)$:**

1. Parse the graph $G$ and the integer $k$ from $x$. Parse the subset $S$ from $y$.
2. **Validity check:** Verify that $\vert S \vert = k$ by counting 1-bits in $y$. If not, **REJECT**. *(Cost: $O(n)$)*
3. **Pairwise edge check:** For every pair of distinct vertices $u, v \in S$ (there are $\binom{k}{2} = O(k^2)$ such pairs), verify that $(u, v) \in E$. If any pair is missing, **REJECT**. *(Cost: $O(k^2 \cdot n)$ in the worst case for adjacency-list lookup)*
4. If all checks pass, **ACCEPT**.

**Running Time:** $T_V = O(n + k^2 \cdot n) = O(n^3)$, which is polynomial in $\vert x \vert = O(n + m)$. **(1.5 marks)**

**Completeness:** If $G$ has a $k$-clique $S^*$, the certificate $y^*$ encoding $S^*$ makes $V$ accept (every pair in $S^*$ is connected by an edge). ✓ **(1 mark)**

**Soundness:** If $V(x, y) = \text{ACCEPT}$, then $S$ (decoded from $y$) has $\vert S \vert = k$ and every pair in $S$ is an edge, so $S$ is a $k$-clique. ✓ **(1 mark)**

**Conclusion:** A polynomial-time verifier exists, so **CLIQUE ∈ NP**. $\blacksquare$ **(1 mark)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Adapted from June 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define the complexity class P using the formal notation of deterministic Turing Machines. Explain with a clear example why polynomial time is considered the "tractability threshold."

**(b)** [7 Marks] Prove that the problem **2-SAT** ∈ P. (Note: 2-SAT is a special case of SAT where every clause has at most 2 literals.)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Definition of P and Polynomial-Time Tractability**

**Definition:** The complexity class **P** is defined as:
$$P = \bigcup_{k \geq 0} DTIME(n^k)$$

where $DTIME(n^k)$ is the set of all languages $L \subseteq \{0,1\}^*$ for which there exists a deterministic Turing Machine $M$ satisfying:
1. $x \in L \iff M$ accepts the input $x$.
2. $M$ halts on input $x$ within $O(\vert x \vert^k)$ steps.

**(3 marks)**

**Why polynomial time = tractability threshold:**

1. **Robustness across models:** Polynomial time is *invariant* across all reasonable models of sequential computation (Turing Machines, RAM machines, modern CPUs) up to polynomial factors. A polynomial-time algorithm on a TM can be translated to a polynomial-time algorithm on a modern computer and vice versa. **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Closure under composition and subroutine calls:** If a polynomial-time algorithm calls another polynomial-time subroutine a constant number of times, the result is still polynomial. This composability is essential for building complex algorithms from simple ones. **(1 mark)**

3. **Cobham-Edmonds Thesis:** An algorithm is considered "efficient" if and only if it runs in polynomial time. This is a community-accepted convention rather than a theorem, but it matches empirical observation: problems with polynomial solutions are practically solvable for reasonable input sizes, while those with exponential solutions quickly become infeasible. **(1.5 marks)**

---

**(b) — Proof that 2-SAT ∈ P**

**Problem Restatement:** Given a Boolean formula $\varphi$ in CNF where every clause contains *at most 2 literals*, determine whether $\varphi$ is satisfiable. **(1 mark)**

**Algorithm — Implication Graph + SCC Reduction (Polynomial-Time Procedure):**

**Step 1 — Construct the Implication Graph $G_\varphi$.**

For each variable $x_i$ in $\varphi$, create two vertices: $x_i$ (true) and $\neg x_i$ (false). For each clause, add two directed edges:
- Clause $(x_i \vee x_j)$: add edge $\neg x_i \rightarrow x_j$ and $\neg x_j \rightarrow x_i$.
- Clause $(\neg x_i \vee x_j)$: add edge $x_i \rightarrow x_j$ and $\neg x_j \rightarrow \neg x_i$.
- Clause $(x_i)$: add edge $\neg x_i \rightarrow x_i$.
- Clause $(\neg x_i)$: add edge $x_i \rightarrow \neg x_i$.

The graph has $2n$ vertices and at most $2m$ edges, where $n$ is the number of variables and $m$ the number of clauses. **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Compute Strongly Connected Components (SCCs).** Use **Tarjan's algorithm** (or Kosaraju's algorithm), which runs in $O(V + E)$ time. Here $V = 2n$ and $E \leq 2m$, so this step takes $O(n + m) = \text{poly}(\vert x \vert)$ time. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — Check for Contradictory Pairs.** For each variable $x_i$, if $x_i$ and $\neg x_i$ are in the same SCC, then there is a directed path from $x_i$ to $\neg x_i$ and vice versa. This means $x_i \iff \neg x_i$, which is a contradiction. **REJECT** in this case. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Construct a Satisfying Assignment.** If no contradiction is found, process SCCs in *reverse topological order* (a topological sort of the SCC DAG). For each SCC, assign all its literals to FALSE initially, then flip to TRUE for those whose negation's SCC has been processed earlier. This assignment satisfies $\varphi$. **ACCEPT**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Running Time Summary:**
- Building $G_\varphi$: $O(n + m)$
- Computing SCCs: $O(n + m)$
- Contradiction check: $O(n)$
- Topological sort + assignment: $O(n + m)$
- **Total:** $O(n + m) = \text{poly}(\vert x \vert)$, a deterministic polynomial.

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** We have constructed a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm for 2-SAT. Therefore:
$$2\text{-SAT} \in DTIME(\text{poly}(n)) = P \qquad \blacksquare$$
**(0.5 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Contrast with 3-SAT:** 3-SAT (clauses of size 3) is **NP-complete** (Cook-Levin theorem, 1971). The jump from 2 to 3 literals per clause transforms a polynomial problem into an NP-complete one — a striking illustration of how a seemingly minor change in problem specification drastically changes complexity.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in P/NP Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "decidable" with "in P":** Every problem in P is decidable, but not every decidable problem is in P. Stopping times, halting, and many arithmetic problems are decidable but not in P (some are undecidable). Always specify the *time bound*, not just the *computability*.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the certificate:** When proving $L \in NP$, students often write the verifier but forget to specify what the certificate $y$ is. Examiners explicitly look for the certificate: *"What is the witness, and why is it polynomial in length?"*
>
> 3. **Writing $O(2^n)$ and calling it polynomial:** Exponential upper bounds are *not* polynomial. $n^{100}$ is polynomial; $1.0001^n$ is not. Be precise.
>
> 4. **Confusing P with NP-hard:** Showing a problem is NP-hard does NOT mean it is in NP. NP-hard problems may not even be in NP (e.g., the Halting Problem is undecidable, let alone in NP). The intersection of NP and NP-hard is **NP-complete**.
>
> 5. **Skipping the soundness/completeness check:** A verifier proof must show *both* directions: (i) if $x \in L$, some certificate works (completeness), and (ii) if some certificate works, $x \in L$ (soundness). Skipping either loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 6. **Mixing up DTM and NTM time bounds:** For P, the bound is on the *single* deterministic computation. For NP via NTM, the bound is on *every* branch (the longest branch). This subtlety is a common exam trap.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **P** = languages decidable by a **deterministic** TM in **polynomial time**. Equivalent: $P = \bigcup_k DTIME(n^k)$.
- **NP** = languages decidable by a **nondeterministic** TM in polynomial time, **OR equivalently**, languages whose members have **polynomial-size certificates verifiable in polynomial time**.
- The verifier definition states: $L \in NP \iff \exists V, p: x \in L \iff \exists y, \vert y \vert \leq p(\vert x \vert) \text{ and } V(x,y) = ACCEPT$.
- $P \subseteq NP$ is *trivially* true (DTM ⊆ NTM).
- $P \stackrel{?}{=} NP$ is the **open Millennium Problem** carrying a **$1M USD prize** (Clay Institute).
- **Examples in P:** PATH, 2-SAT, sorting, matrix multiplication, primality (AKS 2002), graph reachability, shortest path (Dijkstra), MST.
- **Examples in NP:** SAT, 3-SAT, HAM-CYCLE, SUBSET-SUM, CLIQUE, INDEPENDENT-SET, GRAPH-COLORING, TSP (decision), VERTEX-COVER.
- **NP-complete problems** (Cook-Levin + Karp): SAT, 3-SAT, HAM-CYCLE, SUBSET-SUM, CLIQUE, VERTEX-COVER, and 15 others.
- **co-NP** = class of languages whose *complements* are in NP; satisfies $L \in$ co-NP $\iff \overline{L} \in$ NP.
- **The certificate must be of polynomial length** $\vert y \vert \leq p(\vert x \vert)$ — this is a non-negotiable part of the NP definition.
- **Decision vs Optimization:** P and NP classify *decision* problems; optimization versions are addressed by searching over decision thresholds.
- **Polynomial-time robustness:** Polytime is preserved under composition, subroutine calls (constant number), and reasonable model changes — this is why it is the right tractability threshold.
- **Membership proofs** in NP follow a fixed template: (1) state the certificate, (2) describe the verifier, (3) bound the verifier's running time, (4) verify completeness and soundness, (5) conclude.
- **The Cook-Levin Theorem (1971):** SAT was the *first* problem proven NP-complete. Every NP problem reduces to SAT in polynomial time.
- **Remember the verifier example:** For COMPOSITE, the certificate is a non-trivial factor; division takes $O((\log N)^2)$ time, which is polynomial in the input length.
- **Engineering impact:** If $P = NP$, modern **public-key cryptography** (RSA, Diffie-Hellman) would collapse, AI planning would become efficient, and mathematical theorem-proving would be automated.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Polynomial-Time Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST864 - COMPUTATIONAL COMPLEXITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Polynomial-Time Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the formal theory of **Computational Complexity**, an algorithm is said to be a **Polynomial-Time Algorithm** if, for any input of size $n$, the number of elementary operations it performs is bounded above by some polynomial function of $n$. Mathematically:

$$T(n) = O(n^k) \quad \text{for some fixed integer } k \geq 0$$

The collection of all decision problems (languages) that can be decided by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time forms the foundational complexity class known as **P** (also written as **PTIME**):

$$\mathbf{P} = \bigcup_{k=1}^{\infty} \text{DTIME}(n^k)$$

Where $\text{DTIME}(n^k)$ denotes the set of languages decidable by a deterministic Turing machine running in at most $c \cdot n^k$ steps for some constant $c > 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Highlight (PECST864 - Module 1):**
> Polynomial-time algorithms are the cornerstone of "tractable" or "feasible" computation. The Cobham–Edmonds thesis (1965) asserts that a problem can be solved by a computer in a practically feasible manner **if and only if** it admits a polynomial-time algorithm.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Search" Intuition

Imagine you are searching for a specific book in a library with $n$ shelves:

| Strategy | Time Behavior | Polynomial? |
|----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Strategy A:** Walk every shelf one by one | $T(n) = n$ | ✅ Yes (linear) |
| **Strategy B:** Use the index catalog, then binary search | $T(n) = \log_2 n$ | ✅ Yes (logarithmic) |
| **Strategy C:** Ask every possible subgroup of visitors if they have it | $T(n) = 2^n$ | ❌ No (exponential) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** A polynomial-time algorithm is one whose running time grows "reasonably" as input grows. Doubling the input size increases the work by only a constant multiplicative factor (e.g., $n^2 \to (2n)^2 = 4n^2$), rather than blowing up catastrophically (e.g., $2^n \to 2^{2n} = 4^n$).

## 1.3 Why Polynomial Time Matters in Engineering

| Domain | Polynomial-Time Application | Complexity |
|--------|----------------------------|------------|
| **VLSI Chip Design** | Routing & layout verification | $O(n \log n)$ to $O(n^3)$ |
| **Network Routing** | Dijkstra's shortest path | $O((V+E) \log V)$ |
| **Cryptography** | RSA encryption (encryption side) | $O(n^3)$ modular exponentiation |
| **Operations Research** | Linear Programming (interior point) | $O(n^{3.5})$ |
| **Compiler Design** | Parsing (CYK algorithm) | $O(n^3)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hierarchy of Polynomial Growth Rates

Polynomial-time algorithms are **graded** by the degree $k$ of the bounding polynomial. Even within the class $\mathbf{P}$, lower-degree polynomials are practically preferred:

$$O(1) \subset O(\log n) \subset O(n) \subset O(n \log n) \subset O(n^2) \subset O(n^3) \subset \cdots \subset O(n^k)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In KTU valuation, when asked "Is this polynomial-time?", accept any $O(n^k)$ bound regardless of $k$. When asked "Is this efficient?", the answer depends on the **degree** $k$ and the **hidden constant** $c$.

## 2.2 Closure Properties of Class P

The complexity class $\mathbf{P}$ has crucial algebraic closure properties that examiners frequently test:

| Property | Statement | Proof Sketch |
|----------|-----------|--------------|
| **Closure under Union** | If $L_1, L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$, then $L_1 \cup L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$ | Simulate both deciders, accept if either accepts |
| **Closure under Intersection** | $L_1 \cap L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$ | Simulate both, accept only if both accept |
| **Closure under Complement** | If $L \in \mathbf{P}$, then $\overline{L} \in \mathbf{P}$ | Flip the accept/reject outcome |
| **Closure under Concatenation** | $L_1 L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$ | DP over split positions of input |
| **Closure under Kleene Star** | $L^* \in \mathbf{P}$ | Dynamic programming over prefix lengths |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closure under complement** is one of the most distinguishing features: $\mathbf{P} = \text{co-}\mathbf{P}$. This is **not** trivially true for $\mathbf{NP}$ (the non-deterministic polynomial class), where $\mathbf{NP} \stackrel{?}{=} \text{co-}\mathbf{NP}$ remains open.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Notes for KTU 2024 |
|---------------|---------------------|--------------------|
| $\mathbf{P}$ | $\bigcup_{k \geq 1} \text{DTIME}(n^k)$ | Deterministic polynomial time |
| $T(n)$ | Worst-case running time | $T(n) = c \cdot n^k + \text{lower order}$ |
| $O(n^k)$ | Upper bound on $T(n)$ | Big-O notation |
| $c$ | Hidden constant | Machine/compiler dependent |
| $k$ | Degree of polynomial | Problem-dependent |
| $\log^k n$ | Polylogarithmic | $\subset O(n)$ for any $k$ |
| $2^{\log^c n}$ | Quasi-polynomial | $\not\subset \mathbf{P}$ formally |
| $\mathbf{P} \subseteq \mathbf{NP}$ | Open: equality or strict? | Unsolved ($n^k$ vs $2^n$ machine gap) |
| $L \leq_p L'$ | Polynomial-time many-one reduction | Foundation of NP-completeness |
| $x \in L$ iff $f(x) \in L'$ | Karp reduction | Computable in $O(n^k)$ |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown Table Warning Honored:** All absolute value and set-membership bars use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the table renderer.

## 2.4 The Robustness Thesis — Invariance Under Model

A remarkable meta-theorem: the class $\mathbf{P}$ is **invariant** under the choice of reasonable deterministic computational model, as long as the model can simulate a standard RAM machine with at most polynomial overhead:

$$T_{\text{Turing}}(n) \leq p(T_{\text{RAM}}(n)) \quad \text{and} \quad T_{\text{RAM}}(n) \leq p(T_{\text{Turing}}(n))$$

This is called the **Extended Church–Turing Thesis** and is what makes $\mathbf{P}$ a *machine-independent* mathematical object.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Compiler Optimization:** Dataflow analysis must terminate; polynomial-time fixed-point iteration guarantees this.
2. **Database Query Engines:** Conjunctive query evaluation is in $\mathbf{P}$ via Yannakakis' algorithm.
3. **Network Protocol Design:** Reachability in bounded-bandwidth networks is polynomial.
4. **Machine Learning:** Kernel SVM training is polynomial; deep learning inference is polynomial but with high constants.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Verifying $O(n^3)$ is Polynomial

**Problem Statement:** Prove that an algorithm running in $T(n) = 5n^3 + 12n^2 - 7n + 42$ steps is a polynomial-time algorithm.

**Step 1 — Apply the definition of Big-O.**
We must find constants $c > 0$ and $n_0 \geq 1$ such that $T(n) \leq c \cdot n^k$ for all $n \geq n_0$.

For $n \geq 1$, each term is bounded:
- $5n^3 \leq 5n^3$
- $12n^2 \leq 12n^3$ (since $n^2 \leq n^3$)
- $7n \leq 7n^3$ (since $n \leq n^3$)
- $42 \leq 42n^3$ (since $1 \leq n^3$)

**Step 2 — Sum the bounds.**

$$T(n) = 5n^3 + 12n^2 - 7n + 42 \leq 5n^3 + 12n^3 + 7n^3 + 42n^3$$

**Step 3 — Combine the coefficients.**

$$T(n) \leq (5 + 12 + 7 + 42) \cdot n^3 = 66 \cdot n^3$$

**Step 4 — Conclude.**

$$T(n) \leq 66 n^3 \quad \text{for all } n \geq 1$$

Therefore $T(n) = O(n^3)$ with witnesses $c = 66$ and $n_0 = 1$. Since the upper bound is a polynomial in $n$, the algorithm is a **polynomial-time algorithm**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Comparing Polynomial vs. Exponential Growth

**Problem Statement:** Show that for sufficiently large $n$, any polynomial $p(n) = n^k$ is dominated by an exponential $2^{n}$, i.e., $n^k \in o(2^n)$.

**Step 1 — Take the limit definition.**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n^k}{2^n} = 0 \quad \text{must be shown.}$$

**Step 2 — Apply repeated L'Hôpital's rule (k times).**

Differentiate the numerator $k$ times until it becomes a constant $k!$:

$$\frac{n^k}{2^n} \quad \xrightarrow{\text{L'H}} \quad \frac{k \cdot n^{k-1}}{2^n \ln 2} \quad \xrightarrow{\text{L'H}} \quad \cdots \quad \xrightarrow{\text{L'H}} \quad \frac{k!}{2^n (\ln 2)^k}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the final limit.**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{k!}{2^n (\ln 2)^k} = 0$$

Since $2^n$ grows without bound in the denominator while $k!$ remains constant, the limit is zero.

**Step 4 — Conclude the Big-O relation.**

$$n^k = o(2^n) \implies n^k \in O(2^n) \setminus \Theta(2^n)$$

This proves that **polynomial growth is strictly slower than exponential growth** asymptotically — the foundation of why $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{EXP}$.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Polynomial-Time Closure under Union

**Theorem:** If $L_1 \in \mathbf{P}$ and $L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$, then $L_1 \cup L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$.

**Proof by Construction:**

*Step 1.* Since $L_1 \in \mathbf{P}$, there exists a deterministic Turing machine $M_1$ deciding $L_1$ in time $O(n^{k_1})$.

*Step 2.* Since $L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$, there exists a deterministic Turing machine $M_2$ deciding $L_2$ in time $O(n^{k_2})$.

*Step 3.* Construct a combined machine $M$ that, on input $x$ of length $n = \vert x \vert$:

$$M(x): \quad \text{Run } M_1(x); \quad \text{if accept, accept}; \quad \text{else run } M_2(x); \quad \text{output } M_2\text{'s result}$$

*Step 4 — Time analysis.* Total time:

$$T_M(n) \leq c_1 n^{k_1} + c_2 n^{k_2}$$

*Step 5 — Bound by a single polynomial.* Let $k = \max(k_1, k_2)$ and observe $n^{k_i} \leq n^k$ for $n \geq 1$:

$$T_M(n) \leq (c_1 + c_2) n^k = c' n^k$$

*Step 6 — Conclusion.* $M$ decides $L_1 \cup L_2$ in time $O(n^k)$, so $L_1 \cup L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Verifying an Algorithm is NOT Polynomial-Time

**Problem Statement:** An algorithm has worst-case running time $T(n) = 2^{\sqrt{n}}$. Determine if this is polynomial-time.

**Step 1 — Recall the definition.** $T(n)$ is polynomial iff $T(n) = O(n^k)$ for some fixed $k$.

**Step 2 — Test the candidate.** Suppose for contradiction $2^{\sqrt{n}} = O(n^k)$ for some fixed $k$. Then there exists $c, n_0$ with:

$$2^{\sqrt{n}} \leq c \cdot n^k \quad \text{for all } n \geq n_0$$

**Step 3 — Take logarithms of both sides.**

$$\sqrt{n} \leq \log_2 c + k \log_2 n$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic comparison.** As $n \to \infty$, the left-hand side $\sqrt{n}$ grows faster than $k \log_2 n$. Therefore, for sufficiently large $n$:

$$\sqrt{n} > k \log_2 n + \log_2 c$$

This contradicts the assumed inequality.

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

$$2^{\sqrt{n}} \notin O(n^k) \quad \text{for any fixed } k$$

The algorithm is **not** polynomial-time. In fact, $2^{\sqrt{n}}$ is **sub-exponential** — it lies strictly between polynomial and exponential. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Function $T(n)$ | In $\mathbf{P}$? | Justification |
|-----------------|:---:|---------------|
| $100$ | ✅ | $O(1) = O(n^0)$ |
| $3 \log_2 n$ | ✅ | $\log n \leq n$ for $n \geq 1$ |
| $7n + 2\sqrt{n}$ | ✅ | $O(n)$ |
| $5n^2 + 100n$ | ✅ | $O(n^2)$ |
| $n^{1000}$ | ✅ (theoretically) | $O(n^{1000})$ — practically infeasible |
| $1.5^n$ | ❌ | Exponential |
| $2^{\sqrt{n}}$ | ❌ | Sub-exponential (faster than polynomial) |
| $n^{\log n}$ | ❌ | Quasi-polynomial, not polynomial |
| $n!$ | ❌ | Super-exponential (Stirling) |
| $n^n$ | ❌ | Double-exponential growth class |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Define the complexity class **P**. Why are polynomial-time algorithms considered "tractable" in computational complexity theory? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The complexity class **P** (Polynomial Time) is defined as the set of all decision problems (languages) that can be decided by a deterministic Turing machine whose running time is bounded by a polynomial in the input size. Formally:

$$\mathbf{P} = \bigcup_{k=1}^{\infty} \text{DTIME}(n^k)$$

Polynomial-time algorithms are considered "tractable" because:
1. **Closure under composition:** Composing two polynomial-time algorithms yields a polynomial-time algorithm, enabling modular problem solving.
2. **Model independence:** The class $\mathbf{P}$ is invariant under the choice of deterministic computational model (Extended Church–Turing Thesis), making it a robust mathematical abstraction.
3. **Practical feasibility for moderate $k$:** For small $k$ (e.g., $k \leq 3$), polynomial-time algorithms run within feasible time bounds for realistic input sizes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (–1 mark deducted):** Students often claim "polynomial = always practical". This is false; $O(n^{100})$ is polynomial but practically infeasible. Use the phrase "theoretically tractable" or "Cobham–Edmonds tractable".

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> State **two** closure properties of the complexity class $\mathbf{P}$. Justify each in one sentence. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Property 1 — Closure under Complement:** If $L \in \mathbf{P}$, then $\overline{L} \in \mathbf{P}$. *Justification:* Given a polynomial-time decider $M$ for $L$, simply construct $M'$ that flips the accept/reject outcome; $M'$ still runs in polynomial time.

**Property 2 — Closure under Union:** If $L_1, L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$, then $L_1 \cup L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$. *Justification:* Run both deciders $M_1$ and $M_2$ sequentially on input $x$ and accept if either accepts; the total time $O(n^{k_1}) + O(n^{k_2}) = O(n^{\max(k_1,k_2)})$ remains polynomial.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake (–1 mark):** Writing "P is closed under complement because P = co-P" without constructing the decider. The examiner expects the **construction** argument.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### 🔹 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Define polynomial-time algorithm. Prove that if $L_1 \in \mathbf{P}$ and $L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$, then $L_1 \cap L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Consider three algorithms with running times $T_1(n) = 2^{\log_2 n}$, $T_2(n) = 2^{n}$, and $T_3(n) = n^{1.5}$. Classify each as polynomial-time or not, with rigorous justification.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Definition:** A polynomial-time algorithm is one whose worst-case running time $T(n)$ satisfies $T(n) = O(n^k)$ for some fixed integer $k \geq 0$.

**Proof of Closure under Intersection:**

*Step 1.* Since $L_1 \in \mathbf{P}$, there exists a DTM $M_1$ deciding $L_1$ in time $c_1 \cdot n^{k_1}$.

*Step 2.* Since $L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$, there exists a DTM $M_2$ deciding $L_2$ in time $c_2 \cdot n^{k_2}$.

*Step 3.* Construct the combined machine $M$:

$$M(x) : \text{Run } M_1(x) \to r_1; \quad \text{Run } M_2(x) \to r_2; \quad \text{If } r_1 = \text{accept AND } r_2 = \text{accept, accept; else reject.}$$

*Step 4 — Time Bound:*

$$T_M(n) = T_{M_1}(n) + T_{M_2}(n) \leq c_1 n^{k_1} + c_2 n^{k_2}$$

Let $k = \max(k_1, k_2)$. Then for $n \geq 1$:

$$T_M(n) \leq c_1 n^k + c_2 n^k = (c_1 + c_2) n^k$$

*Step 5.* Since $T_M(n) = O(n^k)$, machine $M$ is polynomial-time. Therefore $L_1 \cap L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Algorithm 1: $T_1(n) = 2^{\log_2 n}$**

Simplify: $2^{\log_2 n} = n$. Therefore $T_1(n) = n = O(n^1)$.

**Conclusion:** Polynomial-time (linear). ✅

**Algorithm 2: $T_2(n) = 2^{n}$**

Suppose for contradiction $2^n = O(n^k)$ for some fixed $k$. Taking $\log_2$:

$$n \leq k \log_2 n + \log_2 c$$

The LHS grows linearly, the RHS grows logarithmically — contradiction for large $n$.

**Conclusion:** Not polynomial-time. ❌

**Algorithm 3: $T_3(n) = n^{1.5}$**

This is already in the form $O(n^k)$ with $k = 1.5$.

**Conclusion:** Polynomial-time (degree 1.5). ✅

**Final Classification Table:**

| Algorithm | Running Time | Polynomial? | Degree $k$ |
|-----------|--------------|:-----------:|:----------:|
| $T_1(n) = 2^{\log_2 n}$ | $n$ | ✅ Yes | $k=1$ |
| $T_2(n) = 2^n$ | $2^n$ | ❌ No | — |
| $T_3(n) = n^{1.5}$ | $n^{1.5}$ | ✅ Yes | $k=1.5$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students will write $2^{\log_2 n} = n$ correctly but forget to express it in $O(n^k)$ form. Always **explicitly write** the resulting polynomial bound to earn full 7 marks.

---

### 🔹 Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **Cobham–Edmonds Thesis**. State and prove the closure of $\mathbf{P}$ under the concatenation of languages.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] An algorithm has time complexity $T(n) = n^3 + n^2 \log n + 100n + 50$. Find the tight Big-O bound. If another algorithm solves the same problem in $T'(n) = 2^{n/10}$, determine for $n = 100$ which algorithm is faster numerically.

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Cobham–Edmonds Thesis (1965):**
A computational problem is feasibly solvable by a physical computing device **if and only if** it admits a polynomial-time algorithm. This thesis justifies treating $\mathbf{P}$ as the formal embodiment of "practical computability".

**Theorem — Closure under Concatenation:** If $L_1, L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$, then $L_1 L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$.

**Proof:**

*Step 1.* By hypothesis, $L_1$ and $L_2$ are decided by DTMs $M_1$ and $M_2$ in times $O(n^{k_1})$ and $O(n^{k_2})$.

*Step 2.* A string $w \in L_1 L_2$ iff $w = xy$ for some split with $x \in L_1$ and $y \in L_2$.

*Step 3.* Construct $M$ that iterates over all $n+1$ split positions $i = 0, 1, \ldots, n$:

$$\text{For each } i: \text{test if } w[1..i] \in L_1 \text{ AND } w[i+1..n] \in L_2$$

*Step 4 — Time Bound:*

$$T_M(n) \leq \sum_{i=0}^{n} \left[ c_1 i^{k_1} + c_2 (n-i)^{k_2} \right] \leq (n+1)(c_1 n^{k_1} + c_2 n^{k_2}) = O(n^{k+1})$$

where $k = \max(k_1, k_2)$. This is a polynomial.

*Step 5.* Therefore $L_1 L_2 \in \mathbf{P}$. $\blacksquare$

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Part (i) — Tight Big-O Bound:**

$$T(n) = n^3 + n^2 \log n + 100n + 50$$

The dominant term is $n^3$ (highest polynomial degree). For $n \geq 1$:

$$n^3 + n^2 \log n + 100n + 50 \leq n^3 + n^3 + 100n^3 + 50n^3 = 152 n^3$$

**Tight bound:** $T(n) = O(n^3)$, and the bound is tight because $T(n) = \Omega(n^3)$.

**Part (ii) — Numerical Comparison at $n = 100$:**

For the polynomial algorithm:

$$T(100) = 100^3 + 100^2 \log 100 + 100 \cdot 100 + 50 = 1{,}000{,}000 + 10{,}000 \cdot 2 + 10{,}050 \approx 1{,}020{,}050$$

For the exponential algorithm:

$$T'(100) = 2^{100/10} = 2^{10} = 1024$$

**Numerical comparison at $n = 100$:** The exponential algorithm is *numerically* faster at this small input size ($1024 < 1{,}020{,}050$).

**However**, asymptotically, $T(n) = O(n^3)$ and $T'(n) = 2^{n/10} = 2^{0.1 n}$. There exists a crossover point beyond which the polynomial algorithm wins permanently. Setting $n^3 = 2^{0.1 n}$:

$$3 \log_2 n = 0.1 n \implies \text{crossover around } n \approx 7000 \text{ to } 10000$$

**Conclusion:** The polynomial algorithm is **asymptotically superior**, but for $n = 100$, the exponential is numerically faster. This illustrates that Big-O describes *asymptotic* behavior, not small-input performance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (–2 marks):** Students often conclude that "polynomial is always faster than exponential". The correct conclusion is that polynomial is asymptotically faster; for small $n$, constants can dominate. Be precise!

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Polynomial-Time Algorithms**

- ✅ **Definition:** $T(n) = O(n^k)$ for some fixed integer $k \geq 0$.
- ✅ **Class $\mathbf{P}$:** $\mathbf{P} = \bigcup_{k \geq 1} \text{DTIME}(n^k)$ — set of languages decidable in polynomial time.
- ✅ **Cobham–Edmonds Thesis:** $\mathbf{P}$ represents "feasible" computation.
- ✅ **Closure Properties:** $\mathbf{P}$ is closed under union, intersection, complement, concatenation, and Kleene star.
- ✅ **Co-class:** $\mathbf{P} = \text{co-}\mathbf{P}$ (deterministic symmetry).
- ✅ **Containment:** $\mathbf{P} \subseteq \mathbf{NP} \subseteq \text{PSPACE} \subseteq \text{EXPTIME}$.
- ✅ **Model Invariance:** $\mathbf{P}$ is robust under deterministic model changes (Extended Church–Turing).
- ✅ **Polynomial Hierarchy:** $O(1) \subset O(\log n) \subset O(n) \subset O(n \log n) \subset O(n^2) \subset O(n^k)$.
- ✅ **NOT Polynomial:** $2^n$, $n!$, $n^n$, $2^{\sqrt{n}}$, $n^{\log n}$ — these grow faster than any polynomial.
- ✅ **Key Examples of $\mathbf{P}$:** Sorting, shortest path, MST, matrix multiplication, linear programming, reachability in undirected graphs.
- ✅ **Reduction Tool:** Polynomial-time many-one reduction $\leq_p$ is the foundation of NP-completeness proofs.
- ✅ **Common Pitfall:** $n^{1000}$ is polynomial but not practically feasible. Always distinguish *theoretical* tractability from *engineering* practicality.
- ✅ **Examiner's Mantra:** "Always state the polynomial degree $k$ explicitly in Big-O answers."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NP-Completeness and the Cook-Levin Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST864 - COMPUTATIONAL COMPLEXITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** NP-Completeness and the Cook-Levin Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: NP-COMPLETENESS AND THE COOK-LEVIN THEOREM

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of NP-Completeness

A decision problem (language) $L$ is called **NP-Complete** if and only if it satisfies the following two simultaneous conditions, as defined in the KTU 2024 Computational Complexity syllabus:

1. **Membership in NP:** $L \in \mathbf{NP}$ — i.e., every "yes"-instance of $L$ has a certificate (witness) that can be *verified* in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine.
2. **NP-Hardness:** $L$ is **NP-Hard** — i.e., for *every* language $L' \in \mathbf{NP}$, there exists a polynomial-time many-one reduction $L' \leq_{p} L$.

Equivalently, a language $L$ is NP-Complete if:

$$\forall L' \in \mathbf{NP}, \quad L' \leq_{p} L \quad \wedge \quad L \in \mathbf{NP}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** NP-Completeness is a *property of decision problems* (problems whose answer is YES or NO). Optimization versions (e.g., finding the *shortest* tour in TSP) must first be cast into their decision form before NP-Completeness can be discussed.

### 1.2 Formal Statement of the Cook-Levin Theorem

The **Cook-Levin Theorem** (1971) is the foundational result that bootstrapped the entire theory of NP-Completeness:

> **Theorem (Cook, 1971; Levin, 1973):** *The Boolean Satisfiability Problem (**SAT**) is NP-Complete.*

This means:
- $\text{SAT} \in \mathbf{NP}$ (a satisfying assignment is a polynomial-time verifiable certificate).
- For every $L \in \mathbf{NP}$, $L \leq_{p} \text{SAT}$.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are grading a giant crossword puzzle competition:

- **P-class problems** are puzzles you can *solve quickly* in polynomial time (like a Sudoku where you can deduce each cell logically).
- **NP-class problems** are puzzles that, once a clever student hands in a *claimed solution*, you can *check* very quickly whether it is correct — even if finding that solution from scratch is hard. The certificate is the student's filled-in answer sheet, and the verifier is your grading pen.
- **NP-Complete problems** are the "hardest" puzzles in this *verifiable* category. If you could solve *any one* of them quickly (in polynomial time), you could solve *all of them* quickly. They form the "ceiling" of the NP class.
- **The Cook-Levin Theorem** is the discovery that there is *one* canonical NP-Complete puzzle — the **Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT)** — which serves as the "Rosetta Stone" from which *every* other NP-Complete problem is derived via polynomial-time reduction.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Question (P vs NP):** Cook-Levin did not *prove* that $P \neq NP$. It only established SAT as the "anchor" NP-Complete problem. Whether $P = NP$ remains the most celebrated open problem in theoretical computer science, carrying a **USD 1,000,000 Clay Millennium Prize**.

### 1.4 Key Standard Metrics and Quantities (in **bold**)

- **Time Complexity Class P:** Decision problems solvable in deterministic polynomial time $O(n^k)$ for some constant $k \geq 0$.
- **Time Complexity Class NP:** Decision problems whose "yes"-instances admit certificates verifiable in deterministic polynomial time.
- **Polynomial-Time Reduction ($ \leq_{p} $):** A function $f$ computable in $O(n^k)$ polynomial time such that $x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2$.
- **Boolean Formula Size ($\vert \phi \vert$):** The number of symbols in the formula $\phi$, which for a reduction from a Turing machine run of length $n^k$ becomes polynomially bounded as $O(n^{ck})$ for some constant $c$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of an NP-Completeness Proof (Two Mandatory Steps)

To prove that a problem $X$ is NP-Complete, the KTU examiner expects *both* of the following steps:

### Step 1 — Show $X \in \mathbf{NP}$
- Designate a concise certificate (polynomial in input size).
- Provide a deterministic polynomial-time verifier algorithm $V$ that, given (instance, certificate), accepts iff the certificate is valid.
- Confirm the verifier's runtime is $O(n^c)$ for some constant $c$.

### Step 2 — Show $X$ is NP-Hard
- Choose a known NP-Complete problem $Y$.
- Construct a polynomial-time computable function $f$ such that for every input $w$:

$$w \in Y \iff f(w) \in X$$

- This is the **many-one polynomial-time reduction** $Y \leq_{p} X$.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** When picking $Y$ in Step 2, choose the source problem whose *structure most resembles* $X$. For graph problems, reduce from **3-SAT, Independent Set, or Hamiltonian Cycle**. For number problems, reduce from **Subset Sum**. This minimizes the construction effort and earns full marks.

## 2.2 The Boolean Satisfiability Problem Family

The Cook-Levin Theorem deals with three closely related problems:

### 2.2.1 SAT (General Boolean Satisfiability)
Given a Boolean formula $\phi$ over variables $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ using $\wedge, \vee, \neg$, does there exist an assignment $a \in \{0, 1\}^n$ such that $\phi(a) = 1$ (True)?

### 2.2.2 CNF-SAT
$\phi$ is in **Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF)**: a conjunction ($\wedge$) of *clauses*, each clause being a disjunction ($\vee$) of *literals* (variable or its negation).

$$\phi = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{m} \left( \bigvee_{j \in C_i} \ell_{ij} \right)$$

### 2.2.3 3-SAT
Every clause contains **exactly three literals**. Cook's theorem actually yields CNF-SAT (and by a standard local-replacement gadget, 3-SAT is also NP-Complete).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula, definition, and reduction identity required for Module 1 problems. All notations use $\vert \cdot \vert$ for cardinality, written as `\vert \cdot \vert` to preserve markdown.

| **S.No.** | **Concept** | **Formal Statement / Formula** | **Engineering / Mathematical Utility** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Polynomial-Time Reduction | $L_1 \leq_{p} L_2$ iff $\exists f$ computable in $O(n^k)$ such that $x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2$ | The fundamental tool to *propagate* NP-Completeness across problems. |
| 2 | Class P | $P = \bigcup_{k \geq 0} \text{DTIME}(n^k)$ | Efficiently solvable problems — the engineering ideal. |
| 3 | Class NP | $L \in NP$ iff $\exists$ poly-time verifier $V$ with $L = \{ x \mid \exists y, \vert y \vert \leq \vert x \vert^{O(1)}, V(x,y)=1 \}$ | Verifiable-in-principle problems. |
| 4 | Cook-Levin Core Identity | $\forall L \in NP, \quad L \leq_{p} \text{SAT}$ | Establishes SAT as the canonical NP-Complete anchor. |
| 5 | Transitivity of Reductions | $A \leq_{p} B \;\wedge\; B \leq_{p} C \implies A \leq_{p} C$ | Allows chaining reductions: SAT $\to$ 3-SAT $\to$ CLIQUE $\to$ INDEPENDENT SET $\to$ VERTEX COVER. |
| 6 | Boolean Clause Count Bound | If TM $M$ runs in $n^k$ steps on input $x$, then $\vert \phi_{M,x} \vert = O(n^{ck})$ literals for constant $c$ | Guarantees the constructed SAT formula is *polynomially sized*. |
| 7 | Pumping Length (cross-ref) | For CFL pumping length $p$, formula length $\vert \phi \vert \leq p \cdot \vert x \vert^{ck}$ | Used in NP-Completeness proofs of CFL membership questions. |
| 8 | Decision Problem Conversion | $\text{OPT}(I) \leq K \iff \text{DECIDE}(I, K) = $ YES | Standard way to convert optimization $\to$ decision for NP-Completeness. |
| 9 | Complement Class co-NP | $\overline{L} \in $ co-NP iff $L \in $ co-NP | Relevant for UN-SAT, TAUTOLOGY (open if co-NP = NP). |
| 10 | Karp vs Turing Reductions | Karp: $x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2$; Turing: poly-time oracle calls | KTU syllabus uses **Karp** (many-one) reductions exclusively. |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of NP-Completeness

- **Cryptography:** Modern public-key systems (RSA, AES) rely on the *belief* $P \neq NP$. If $P = NP$, factoring (currently NP-Hard under randomized reductions) would be in P, breaking RSA.
- **Operations Research:** TSP, Vehicle Routing, Job-Shop Scheduling — recognizing NP-Completeness tells engineers to use *approximation algorithms* (e.g., Christofides' 1.5-approximation for TSP) or *heuristics* (simulated annealing, genetic algorithms) rather than seeking exact polynomial solutions.
- **VLSI Design:** Circuit Satisfiability and Physical Mapping problems are NP-Complete; CAD tools therefore deploy SAT-solvers (DPLL, CDCL) which perform well in practice despite worst-case exponential behavior.
- **Bioinformatics:** Protein Folding, Phylogenetic Tree Reconstruction — NP-Complete problems solved via probabilistic and heuristic methods in genomics pipelines.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Proof Sketch of the Cook-Levin Theorem

The Cook-Levin Theorem is the *only* NP-Completeness result in the entire theory that must be proved "from first principles" (i.e., directly from the definition of NP, without relying on a previously known NP-Complete problem). All subsequent NP-Completeness results (3-SAT, CLIQUE, SUBSET-SUM, etc.) are *corollaries* of this single theorem, plus additional polynomial-time reductions.

### 3.1.1 The High-Level Construction Strategy

**Setup:** Let $L \in \mathbf{NP}$ be an arbitrary NP language. By the verifier definition, there exists a deterministic polynomial-time Turing machine $V$ and a polynomial $p(\cdot)$ such that:

$$L = \left\{ x \in \{0,1\}^{*} \;\Big|\; \exists y \in \{0,1\}^{p(\vert x \vert)}, \quad V(x, y) = 1 \right\}$$

$V$ runs in time at most $p(n)$ on inputs of length $n = \vert x \vert$. We must construct, in time polynomial in $n$, a Boolean formula $\phi_{x}$ such that:

$$\exists y, \; V(x, y) = 1 \iff \phi_{x} \text{ is satisfiable}$$

The certificate $y$ will be "absorbed" into the satisfying assignment of $\phi_x$. The construction has **six carefully synchronized components**.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — The Computation Tableau

Represent the entire execution of $V(x, y)$ as a two-dimensional **computation tableau** $T$. This tableau is a matrix whose rows correspond to time steps of $V$ and whose columns correspond to tape cells.

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \text{Tableau of size } (p(n) + 1) \times (p(n) + 1) \\
\text{Number of cells} \;&=\; (p(n)+1)^2 \;=\; O(n^{2k}) \quad \text{where } p(n) = n^k
\end{aligned}$$

Each cell $T[i][j]$ stores a symbol from the finite tape alphabet $\Gamma$ of $V$, *or* the boundary marker $\#$. We introduce a Boolean variable $x_{i,j,s}$ for every $1 \leq i,j \leq p(n)+1$ and every $s \in \Gamma \cup \{\#\}$:

$$x_{i,j,s} = 1 \iff \text{At time step } i, \text{ tape cell } j \text{ contains symbol } s$$

**Number of Boolean variables introduced:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{vars}} &= (p(n)+1)^2 \cdot \vert \Gamma \cup \{\#\} \vert \\
                &= O(n^{2k}) \cdot \vert \Gamma \vert \\
                &= O(n^{2k})
\end{aligned}$$

This is **polynomial in $n$**, satisfying the reduction's size bound.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — The Cell Constraint $\phi_{\text{cell}}$

We must enforce that *each cell contains exactly one symbol* at each time step. This is encoded as a conjunction over all cells:

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi_{\text{cell}} &= \bigwedge_{i=1}^{p(n)+1} \bigwedge_{j=1}^{p(n)+1} \Bigg[ \underbrace{\left( \bigvee_{s} x_{i,j,s} \right)}_{\text{at least one symbol}} \;\wedge\; \underbrace{\bigwedge_{s \neq t} (\neg x_{i,j,s} \vee \neg x_{i,j,t})}_{\text{at most one symbol}} \Bigg]
\end{aligned}$$

The "at most one" clause can be expanded into a CNF sub-formula. The number of clauses per cell is $\binom{\vert \Gamma \vert}{2}$, a constant depending only on $V$ (not on $n$). Total clause count:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{clauses}}^{\text{cell}} &= (p(n)+1)^2 \cdot \left( \vert \Gamma \vert + \binom{\vert \Gamma \vert}{2} \right) \\
                                  &= O(n^{2k}) \quad \text{(polynomial)}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — The Initial Configuration $\phi_{\text{start}}$

The first row (time step $i = 1$) must encode the input tape: the symbol $x_1 x_2 \dots x_n$ (the input), the symbol $y_1 y_2 \dots y_{p(n)}$ (the certificate, padded with blanks), and the rest of the tape being blank $\sqcup$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi_{\text{start}} &= x_{1, 1, x_1} \;\wedge\; x_{1, 2, x_2} \;\wedge\; \cdots \;\wedge\; x_{1, n, x_n} \\
                    &\quad\wedge\; x_{1, n+1, y_1} \;\wedge\; \cdots \;\wedge\; x_{1, n+p(n), y_{p(n)}} \\
                    &\quad\wedge\; \bigwedge_{j \notin [1, n+p(n)]} x_{1, j, \sqcup}
\end{aligned}$$

This is a conjunction of $(p(n)+1)$ literals — i.e., a 1-CNF (Horn-like) formula, trivially convertible to CNF.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — The Accepting Configuration $\phi_{\text{accept}}$

We force at least one cell in the last row to contain the accept state $q_{\text{accept}}$:

$$\phi_{\text{accept}} = \bigvee_{j=1}^{p(n)+1} x_{p(n)+1, j, q_{\text{accept}}}$$

This is a single clause of size $p(n)+1$, so it is *not* in 3-CNF. We will address this in Step 7 (CNF conversion via introduction of auxiliary variables).

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — The Transition Constraints $\phi_{\text{move}}$

For each time step $i = 1, 2, \dots, p(n)$, we must ensure that the configuration at time $i+1$ is obtained from the configuration at time $i$ by a *legal move* of $V$'s transition function $\delta$. A $2 \times 3$ "window" of cells in rows $i$ and $i+1$ is sufficient to verify the local correctness of a single step.

$$\phi_{\text{move}} = \bigwedge_{i=1}^{p(n)} \bigwedge_{j=1}^{p(n)} \bigvee_{(a_1, a_2, a_3, b_1, b_2, b_3) \in \text{ValidWindows}} \left( x_{i, j, a_1} \wedge x_{i, j+1, a_2} \wedge x_{i, j+2, a_3} \wedge x_{i+1, j, b_1} \wedge x_{i+1, j+1, b_2} \wedge x_{i+1, j+2, b_3} \right)$$

Here, $\text{ValidWindows}$ is the set of 6-tuples consistent with $\delta$. The number of valid windows is a *constant* depending only on $V$ (since $\vert Q \vert$, $\vert \Gamma \vert$, and the number of transitions in $\delta$ are all constants of the fixed verifier $V$). Each window yields a 6-literal clause, so each $(i,j)$ contributes a disjunction of constant-many 6-literal conjunctions. After applying distributive law:

$$N_{\text{clauses}}^{\text{move}} = O(p(n)^2) \cdot \text{const} = O(n^{2k})$$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Assembling the Master Formula

The complete Cook-Levin formula is the conjunction of all six sub-formulas:

$$\phi_{x} = \phi_{\text{cell}} \;\wedge\; \phi_{\text{start}} \;\wedge\; \phi_{\text{accept}} \;\wedge\; \phi_{\text{move}}$$

If we accept the verifier is local (window property), we have:

$$\begin{aligned}
\exists y, V(x,y)=1 &\iff \exists \text{ tableau } T \text{ consistent with all 4 constraints} \\
                     &\iff \exists \text{ assignment to } x_{i,j,s} \text{ variables satisfying } \phi_x \\
                     &\iff \phi_x \text{ is satisfiable}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Conversion to CNF (Crucial for 3-SAT Reduction)

The clauses in $\phi_{\text{move}}$ and $\phi_{\text{accept}}$ have varying widths (some up to size $p(n)$). Cook's original proof keeps the formula in CNF (with possibly wide clauses). For the 3-SAT version, we apply the **standard Tseitin-like transformation**:

- For each clause $C = (\ell_1 \vee \ell_2 \vee \cdots \vee \ell_w)$ with $w > 3$:
  - Introduce auxiliary variables $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_{w-2}$.
  - Replace with: $(\ell_1 \vee \ell_2 \vee y_1) \wedge (\neg y_1 \vee \ell_3 \vee y_2) \wedge \cdots \wedge (\neg y_{w-2} \vee \ell_{w-1} \vee \ell_w)$.
  - This adds $O(w)$ new variables and $O(w)$ new 3-literal clauses.

Since the original $\phi_x$ has $O(n^{2k})$ symbols and each symbol belongs to at most $O(1)$ wide clauses, the transformation is purely local and yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{vars}}^{\text{3SAT}} &= O(n^{2k}) \\
N_{\text{clauses}}^{\text{3SAT}} &= O(n^{2k})
\end{aligned}$$

So the final 3-CNF formula is **polynomially sized**, completing the reduction.

### 3.1.9 Final Polynomial Bound Verification

The entire construction — variable creation, clause generation, CNF conversion — runs in time:

$$T_{\text{reduction}}(n) = O\left( \vert \phi_x \vert \right) = O(n^{2k} \cdot \vert \Gamma \vert^6) = O(n^{ck})$$

for some constant $c$. This is polynomial, so $L \leq_{p} \text{SAT}$, completing the proof. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 The Analytical Matrix: Reduction Chains for Common NP-Complete Problems

| **From Problem ($L_1$)** | **To Problem ($L_2$)** | **Key Gadget / Idea** | **Clause / Gadget Size** | **Polynomial Bound** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SAT | 3-SAT | Auxiliary variables for wide clauses | $O(\vert \phi \vert)$ | $O(n^{2k})$ |
| 3-SAT | INDEPENDENT SET | Variable gadget: clique; Clause gadget: triple | $\vert V \vert = 2n + 3m$ | $O(n+m)$ |
| INDEPENDENT SET | CLIQUE | Edge complement graph | $\vert V \vert$ unchanged | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ |
| INDEPENDENT SET | VERTEX COVER | $ \vert C \vert = n - \alpha(G) $ complement | $\vert V \vert$ unchanged | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ |
| 3-SAT | SUBSET-SUM | Encode variables and clauses as integers | Numbers of size $O(\text{poly}(n,m))$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| SUBSET-SUM | PARTITION | Add extra element with sum $\sigma$ | One extra integer | $O(n)$ |
| PARTITION | BIN PACKING | Capacity $B$ with item sizes $s_i$ | $O(n)$ items | $O(n)$ |
| 3-SAT | HAMILTONIAN CYCLE | Variable + Clause + Edge gadgets | $O(n + m)$ vertices | $O(n+m)$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complexity Class Hierarchy

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the canonical relationship between P, NP, NP-Complete, and NP-Hard as defined under the Cook-Levin paradigm.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef known fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef complete fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef hard fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef unknown fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000,stroke-dasharray: 5 5

    Pclass["CLASS P\nDeterministic Polynomial Time"]:::known
    NPclass["CLASS NP\nNondeterministic Polynomial Time"]:::known
    NPCclass["NP COMPLETE\nSAT, 3-SAT, CLIQUE, TSP, SUBSET SUM"]:::complete
    NPHclass["NP HARD\nAll NP problems plus harder"]:::hard
    EXPCLASS["EXPONENTIAL TIME\nUnconditionally hard problems"]:::known

    Pclass -->|"subset of"| NPclass
    NPclass -->|"NP Complete sits at the boundary"| NPCclass
    NPclass -->|"NP Hard extends beyond NP"| NPHclass
    NPCclass -->|"intersection of NP and NP Hard"| NPHclass
    NPclass -->|"contained in"| EXPCLASS
    Pclass -->|"contained in"| EXPCLASS
```

> [!NOTE]
> The famous open question "Is P = NP?" corresponds to whether the boundary of `Pclass` coincides with the boundary of `NPclass`. Cook-Levin does not settle this; it only proves that if $P \neq NP$, then no NP-Complete problem lies inside $P$.

## 4.2 Cook-Levin Reduction Pipeline (Block Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *algorithmic architecture* of the Cook-Levin reduction from an arbitrary NP problem $L$ to a Boolean formula $\phi_x$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef input fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,stroke-width:2px
    classDef module fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,stroke-width:3px

    INPUT["INPUT x of length n\nverifier V runs in p of n steps\ncertificate y of length p of n"]:::input
    TAB["MODULE A\nConstruct Tableau T of size\np of n plus 1 by p of n plus 1"]:::module
    VAR["MODULE B\nCreate variables x i j s\none per cell per symbol"]:::module
    CELLC["MODULE C\nAdd Cell Constraint phi cell\nunique symbol per cell"]:::module
    STARTC["MODULE D\nAdd Start Configuration phi start\ninput plus certificate on tape"]:::module
    MOVEC["MODULE E\nAdd Move Constraints phi move\nlocal 2 by 3 window rules"]:::module
    ACCEPTC["MODULE F\nAdd Accept Constraint phi accept\nfinal row has q accept"]:::module
    CNFC["MODULE G\nConvert to CNF via auxiliary vars\nTseitin transformation"]:::module
    OUTPUT["OUTPUT phi x in CNF\nsatisfiable iff V accepts x y"]:::output

    INPUT --> TAB
    TAB --> VAR
    VAR --> CELLC
    CELLC --> STARTC
    STARTC --> MOVEC
    MOVEC --> ACCEPTC
    ACCEPTC --> CNFC
    CNFC --> OUTPUT
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper, Module 1]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Define NP-Completeness. List the two conditions that a decision problem must satisfy to be called NP-Complete.**

**Model Answer:**

A decision problem $L$ (a formal language over $\{0,1\}^*$) is called **NP-Complete** if and only if the following two conditions hold simultaneously:

1. **$L \in \mathbf{NP}$:** Every "yes"-instance $x$ of $L$ possesses a *certificate* (witness string) of length at most $\vert x \vert^{O(1)}$, and there exists a deterministic polynomial-time *verifier* $V$ such that $V(x, \text{cert}) = 1$ iff $\text{cert}$ is a valid witness for $x \in L$.

2. **$L$ is NP-Hard:** For every language $L' \in \mathbf{NP}$, there exists a polynomial-time many-one (Karp) reduction $f$ such that for all $x \in \{0,1\}^*$:

$$x \in L' \iff f(x) \in L$$

In other words, $L'$ can be transformed into $L$ in time polynomial in $\vert x \vert$. $\blacksquare$

> **Examiner's Note:** Both conditions are *necessary*. A problem in NP that is not NP-Hard is merely "in NP", not NP-Complete. A problem that is NP-Hard but not known to be in NP is called "NP-Hard" (e.g., the optimization version of TSP).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary, Module 1]**

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**State the Cook-Levin Theorem. Why is it considered the foundation of the entire theory of NP-Completeness?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Cook-Levin Theorem (1971):** *The Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT) — given a Boolean formula $\phi$ over variables $x_1, \dots, x_n$, determine whether there exists an assignment $a \in \{0,1\}^n$ such that $\phi(a) = 1$ — is NP-Complete.*

It is the *foundation* of NP-Completeness theory for the following reasons:

- It is the **first** problem ever proven NP-Complete, established independently by Stephen Cook (1971) and Leonid Levin (1973).
- Its proof is the **only** NP-Completeness proof that does *not* rely on reducing from a previously known NP-Complete problem; it is derived **directly from the definition of the class NP** by encoding the verifier Turing machine's computation as a Boolean formula.
- Every other NP-Completeness result (3-SAT, CLIQUE, VERTEX COVER, SUBSET-SUM, HAMILTONIAN CYCLE, TSP, etc.) is a *corollary* obtained by reducing SAT to that problem in polynomial time.
- It transformed NP-Completeness from a curiosity into a **universal theory** applicable to thousands of practical problems across computer science, operations research, cryptography, and bioinformatics. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Exam, Module 1, Question A]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define the classes P, NP, and NP-Complete rigorously. Explain the concept of polynomial-time many-one (Karp) reduction. State the Cook-Levin Theorem and outline the high-level strategy of its proof.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Given an arbitrary NP language $L$ with polynomial-time verifier $V$ running in time $p(n)$, describe in detail the **computation tableau** construction used in the Cook-Levin proof. Specify the Boolean variables introduced, the **cell constraints**, and explain why the resulting formula has polynomial size.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

- **Class P:** $P = \bigcup_{k \geq 0} \text{DTIME}(n^k)$ — the set of all decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in time at most $O(n^k)$ for some constant $k \geq 0$.

- **Class NP:** A decision problem $L \subseteq \{0,1\}^*$ is in NP iff there exists a deterministic polynomial-time Turing machine $V$ (the *verifier*) and a polynomial $p$ such that:

$$L = \{ x \in \{0,1\}^* \mid \exists y \in \{0,1\}^{p(\vert x \vert)}, \; V(x,y) = 1 \}$$

The string $y$ is the *certificate* (witness). Equivalently, NP is the set of problems with *nondeterministic* polynomial-time decision procedures, or the set of problems whose solutions are *verifiable* in polynomial time.

- **Class NP-Complete:** $L$ is NP-Complete iff $L \in \mathbf{NP}$ *and* $L$ is NP-Hard (i.e., $\forall L' \in \mathbf{NP}, L' \leq_p L$).

- **Polynomial-Time Many-One (Karp) Reduction:** A function $f: \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^*$ is a polynomial-time many-one reduction from $L_1$ to $L_2$ (written $L_1 \leq_p L_2$) iff:
  1. $f$ is computable in deterministic time polynomial in $\vert x \vert$.
  2. For all $x \in \{0,1\}^*$: $x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2$.

- **Cook-Levin Theorem:** SAT is NP-Complete. (See Section 1.2.)

- **High-Level Strategy:** Given $L \in \mathbf{NP}$ with verifier $V$ running in $p(n)$ steps, construct a Boolean formula $\phi_x$ such that $V$ accepts $(x,y)$ for some $y$ iff $\phi_x$ is satisfiable. The formula encodes the entire computation of $V(x, \cdot)$ as a 2D tableau of polynomial size, with cell, start, accept, and transition constraints.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

*(Refer to the detailed derivation in Section 3.1.2 — 3.1.9 above. The student should reproduce the tableau structure, the variable definition $x_{i,j,s}$, the cell constraint (at-least-one + at-most-one), and the polynomial bound $(p(n)+1)^2 \cdot \vert \Gamma \vert = O(n^{2k})$.)*

**Key Steps the Examiner Expects:**

1. Define the tableau $T$ as a $(p(n)+1) \times (p(n)+1)$ matrix of cells, each holding a tape symbol.
2. Define the variables $x_{i,j,s}$ and explain their semantics.
3. Write the cell constraint enforcing a unique symbol per cell, splitting into "at least one" and "at most one" clauses.
4. Compute the total number of Boolean variables and clauses as $O(n^{2k})$, confirming polynomiality.
5. Briefly link this construction to the overall $\phi_x = \phi_{\text{cell}} \wedge \phi_{\text{start}} \wedge \phi_{\text{accept}} \wedge \phi_{\text{move}}$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Main Exam, Module 1, Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)]**

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** State and explain the Cook-Levin Theorem. Using the construction from the proof, show that the number of Boolean variables and clauses produced in the reduction is polynomial in the input size $n = \vert x \vert$. What role do the *cell constraints* $\phi_{\text{cell}}$ play in the correctness of the construction?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider the language $\text{3-SAT} = \{ \phi \mid \phi \text{ is a satisfiable 3-CNF Boolean formula} \}$. Show that 3-SAT is NP-Complete by:
1. Proving 3-SAT $\in$ NP.
2. Reducing CNF-SAT (which is NP-Complete by Cook-Levin) to 3-SAT in polynomial time. Specify the transformation for handling clauses of width greater than 3.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The Cook-Levin Theorem establishes that SAT is NP-Complete. The construction of $\phi_x$ introduces:
- $(p(n)+1)^2$ tableau cells.
- $\vert \Gamma \cup \{\#\} \vert$ symbols per cell.
- Hence $(p(n)+1)^2 \cdot \vert \Gamma \cup \{\#\} \vert = O(n^{2k} \cdot \text{const}) = O(n^{2k})$ Boolean variables.

The cell constraints $\phi_{\text{cell}}$ consist of, for each cell:
- One "at least one symbol" clause of size $\vert \Gamma \vert$ (constant).
- $\binom{\vert \Gamma \vert}{2}$ "at most one symbol" binary clauses (constant-many).
- Total clauses: $(p(n)+1)^2 \cdot O(1) = O(n^{2k})$.

$\phi_{\text{cell}}$ ensures that *every satisfying assignment of $\phi_x$ corresponds to a legal tableau* — i.e., no cell is left blank and no cell contains contradictory symbols. Without $\phi_{\text{cell}}$, the transition constraints $\phi_{\text{move}}$ could not be reliably interpreted.

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

*(1) 3-SAT $\in$ NP:* The certificate is a satisfying assignment $a \in \{0,1\}^n$ (length $n$, polynomial in input size). The verifier substitutes $a$ into $\phi$ and evaluates each of the $m$ 3-literal clauses; this takes $O(nm) = O(n^3)$ time, which is polynomial.

*(2) Reduction CNF-SAT $\leq_p$ 3-SAT:*

Let $\phi$ be a CNF formula with clauses $C_1, C_2, \dots, C_m$. For each clause $C_i$:

- **Case A: $\vert C_i \vert = 3$:** Keep $C_i$ as-is.

- **Case B: $\vert C_i \vert = 1$, i.e., $C_i = (\ell)$:** Replace with $(\ell \vee y_1 \vee y_2) \wedge (\ell \vee \neg y_1 \vee y_2) \wedge (\ell \vee y_1 \vee \neg y_2) \wedge (\ell \vee \neg y_1 \vee \neg y_2)$, where $y_1, y_2$ are fresh variables. The four 3-clauses are equivalent to $\ell$ being true.

- **Case C: $\vert C_i \vert = 2$, i.e., $C_i = (\ell_1 \vee \ell_2)$:** Replace with $(\ell_1 \vee \ell_2 \vee y) \wedge (\ell_1 \vee \ell_2 \vee \neg y)$.

- **Case D: $\vert C_i \vert \geq 4$:** Introduce fresh variables $y_1^{(i)}, y_2^{(i)}, \dots, y_{\vert C_i \vert - 3}^{(i)}$. Replace the single $w$-clause with the $w-2$ clauses:

$$(\ell_1 \vee \ell_2 \vee y_1) \wedge (\neg y_1 \vee \ell_3 \vee y_2) \wedge (\neg y_2 \vee \ell_4 \vee y_3) \wedge \cdots \wedge (\neg y_{w-3} \vee \ell_{w-1} \vee \ell_w)$$

**Correctness:** If $C_i = (\ell_1 \vee \cdots \vee \ell_w)$ is satisfied by some $\ell_j$ being true, we can set $y_1, \dots, y_{j-2} = 1$ and $y_{j-1}, \dots, y_{w-3} = 0$ to satisfy all $w-2$ new clauses. Conversely, if all new clauses are satisfied, propagating through the chain forces at least one $\ell_j$ to be true (by a careful inductive argument on the chain).

**Polynomiality:** Each original $w$-clause produces $w-2$ new clauses and $w-3$ new variables. Since the original $\phi$ has $O(n)$ symbols, the total expansion is $O(n^2)$ symbols and $O(n^2)$ clauses — both polynomial.

Hence $\text{CNF-SAT} \leq_p \text{3-SAT}$ in polynomial time, and 3-SAT is NP-Complete. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for NP-Completeness Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "$L \in \mathbf{NP}$" half:** Many students only prove NP-Hardness. You must explicitly state the certificate and verifier, and bound the verifier's runtime polynomially.
> 2. **Conflating Karp and Turing reductions:** KTU 2024 expects *many-one (Karp)* reductions. Do not invoke oracle-based or Turing reductions unless explicitly asked.
> 3. **Forgetting transitivity:** When chaining reductions, you must explicitly invoke $A \leq_p B \wedge B \leq_p C \implies A \leq_p C$.
> 4. **Polynomial-time verification is not polynomial-time *solvability*:** Do not confuse the verifier $V$ running in $O(n^k)$ with the problem being in P.
> 5. **Confusing optimization with decision:** NP-Completeness applies to *decision* problems. The optimization version of TSP is NP-Hard, not NP-Complete (its exact complexity classification under NP requires care).
> 6. **Skipping the correctness proof of the gadget:** In a reduction, you must prove *both* directions of "iff": "if $x \in L_1$ then $f(x) \in L_2$" *and* "if $f(x) \in L_2$ then $x \in L_1$". Missing one direction costs 3-4 marks.
> 7. **Omitting the polynomial bound:** Always state the size of $f(x)$ as $O(n^k)$ explicitly. This is a *separate* requirement from the reduction being computable in polynomial time.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1: NP-Completeness and the Cook-Levin Theorem)**

- **NP = { problems with polynomial-time verifiable certificates }** — verification, not solution.
- **NP-Complete = NP $\cap$ NP-Hard** — both membership and universality.
- **Cook-Levin Theorem (1971) = SAT is NP-Complete** — the *first* and *only* "from-scratch" NP-Completeness proof.
- **Proof Strategy:** Encode the verifier's computation as a Boolean formula via a 2D *computation tableau* of size $(p(n)+1)^2 = O(n^{2k})$.
- **Key Variables:** $x_{i,j,s}$ = "cell $(i,j)$ contains symbol $s$ at time $i$".
- **Four Constraints of $\phi_x$:** $\phi_{\text{cell}} \wedge \phi_{\text{start}} \wedge \phi_{\text{accept}} \wedge \phi_{\text{move}}$.
- **Locality Principle:** A single TM step depends only on a $2 \times 3$ window of cells, so transition constraints are *local*.
- **CNF Conversion:** Wide clauses are broken using Tseitin-style auxiliary variables; each $w$-clause becomes $w-2$ new 3-clauses and $w-3$ new variables.
- **Polynomial Bound:** Total variables and clauses are both $O(n^{2k} \cdot \text{const})$, where the constants depend only on the verifier $V$ and its alphabet.
- **3-SAT NP-Completeness:** Follows from CNF-SAT by local replacement (Tseitin transformation).
- **Reduction Chain:** SAT $\to$ 3-SAT $\to$ INDEPENDENT SET $\to$ CLIQUE $\to$ VERTEX COVER (all polynomial-time).
- **Reduction Chain:** SAT $\to$ 3-SAT $\to$ SUBSET-SUM $\to$ PARTITION $\to$ BIN PACKING.
- **Transitivity Lemma:** $A \leq_p B \wedge B \leq_p C \implies A \leq_p C$ — used to compose reductions.
- **Karp vs Turing:** KTU uses *Karp* (many-one) reductions exclusively.
- **P vs NP:** Cook-Levin *does not* resolve P vs NP; it only establishes SAT as NP-Complete.
- **Verifier Runtime:** $|y| \leq |x|^{O(1)}$ and $V$ runs in $|x|^{O(1)}$ steps.
- **Computation Tableau Dimensions:** Rows = time steps; Columns = tape positions.
- **Cell Constraint Decomposition:** "at least one symbol" (size $\vert \Gamma \vert$) + "at most one symbol" (size 2).
- **Engineering Impact:** NP-Completeness guides the use of approximation algorithms, heuristics, and SAT-solvers (CDCL) in real-world engineering systems.
- **Historical Note:** Stephen Cook (1971) and Leonid Levin (1973) proved the theorem independently; the problem is sometimes called the "Cook-Levin Theorem" in recognition of both contributions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reductions and Completeness: Polynomial-Time Reductions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST864 - COMPUTATIONAL COMPLEXITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Reductions and Completeness: Polynomial-Time Reductions # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **polynomial-time reduction** is a formal algorithmic transformation used in computational complexity theory to relate the intrinsic difficulty of two decision problems. Formally, a language $L_1 \subseteq \Sigma^*$ is **polynomial-time many-one reducible** to a language $L_2 \subseteq \Sigma^*$ (denoted $L_1 \le_p L_2$) if there exists a **deterministic Turing machine (DTM)** $M$ and a polynomial $p(n)$ such that for every input string $x \in \Sigma^*$: $$ M \text{ halts on } x \text{ in at most } p(\vert x \vert) \text{ steps, and } x \in L_1 \iff M(x) \in L_2 $$ The function $f : \Sigma^* \rightarrow \Sigma^*$ computed by $M$ is called the **reduction function** or **transformation function**. The polynomial-time computability of $f$ guarantees the reduction is **efficient** and respects the complexity class boundary. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Polynomial-time reductions are the **central machinery** for defining complexity classes like $\mathcal{NP}$-completeness, $\mathcal{P}$-completeness, and PSPACE-completeness. Every completeness result in this course is anchored to a specific reduction type. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translation Layer" Intuition Imagine you are a **chess grandmaster** who has never played the game of *Checkers* (Draughts). You want to determine whether Checkers is a "hard" game. Rather than learning Checkers from scratch, you find a **translator** who can convert every Checkers board position into an *equivalent* Chess position such that: - **Checkers has a winning strategy** $\iff$ **Chess has a winning strategy**. - The translation takes **bounded time** (polynomial effort). If such a translator exists, and Chess is already known to be hard, then Checkers **must also be hard**. Conversely, if someone discovers a fast winning strategy for Checkers, then Chess automatically becomes solvable too. This is precisely what a polynomial-time reduction does: it acts as a **polynomial-time translator** between two problems, preserving the yes/no answer. | Aspect | Real-World Equivalent | Computational Counterpart | |---|---|---| | Translator | Human interpreter | Turing Machine $M$ computing $f$ | | Bounded Effort | Quick, rule-based translation | $\mathcal{O}(p(n))$ time complexity | | Logical Equivalence | Winning strategy preserved | $x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2$ | | Known Hardness | Chess difficulty | Membership in $\mathcal{NP}$-hard class | > [!NOTE] > **Definition (Polynomial-Time Computable Function):** A function $f : \Sigma^* \rightarrow \Sigma^*$ is polynomial-time computable if there exists a polynomial-time DTM that, on input $x$, writes $f(x)$ on its tape and halts. --- ## 1.3 Variants of Polynomial-Time Reductions (KTU Taxonomy) The KTU 2024 syllabus distinguishes between three principal variants: 1. **Many-One Reduction ($\le_m^p$ or $\le_p$):** The standard reduction where $f(x)$ maps a single instance to a single instance. Used for **$\mathcal{NP}$-completeness**. 2. **Turing Reduction ($\le_T^p$):** Allows the reduction algorithm to make **multiple queries** (oracle calls) to the target problem. More powerful than many-one; used in **function problems** and counting complexity ($\#P$). 3. **Truth-Table Reduction ($\le_{tt}^p$):** A restricted form of Turing reduction where all queries must be computed in advance as a "truth table" of bounded size. > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall Alert:** Many-one reductions are **transitive** ($A \le_p B$ and $B \le_p C$ implies $A \le_p C$). Turing reductions are also transitive. However, **log-space reductions** (often confused in textbooks) are stricter and not always transitive under the same conditions. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Anatomy of a Polynomial-Time Reduction A polynomial-time reduction is **not** a "magic trick" — it has strict operational constraints that every KTU examiner expects you to state explicitly. ### Structural Decomposition - **Step 1 — Instance Mapping:** The reduction algorithm $M$ takes an arbitrary instance $x$ of source problem $L_1$ as input. - **Step 2 — Polynomial-Time Transformation:** $M$ constructs a new instance $y = f(x)$ of the target problem $L_2$ using at most $p(\vert x \vert)$ deterministic steps. - **Step 3 — Equivalence Preservation:** The yes/no answer of $x \in L_1$ must exactly match the yes/no answer of $y \in L_2$. - **Step 4 — Polynomial Verifiability:** $f$ must itself be a polynomial-time *computable* function (not merely polynomially bounded in output size, though this is implied). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Polynomial-Time and Not Just Any Time Bound?** > The choice of polynomial time is **not arbitrary**. The **Extended Church-Turing Thesis** + **Cobham's Thesis** identifies polynomial time as the formal notion of *tractable computation*. Any super-polynomial bound (e.g., exponential) would defeat the purpose: if solving $L_2$ takes exponential time, then reducing $L_1$ to $L_2$ yields no useful algorithmic speedup. ## 2.2 The Logical Force of Reductions The central implication that KTU examiners test is: $$ L_1 \le_p L_2 \implies \text{if } L_2 \in \mathcal{P}, \text{ then } L_1 \in \mathcal{P} $$ The **contrapositive** (used in $\mathcal{NP}$-hardness proofs): $$ L_1 \le_p L_2 \text{ and } L_1 \notin \mathcal{P} \implies L_2 \notin \mathcal{P} $$ This second form is what makes reductions the **weapons of choice** for proving hardness: assume $L_1$ is hard, then any problem $L_2$ that $L_1$ reduces to must also be hard. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Polynomial-Time Reductions | Symbol / Concept | Formal Statement | Operational Meaning | |---|---|---| | $L_1 \le_p L_2$ | $L_1$ polynomial-time many-one reduces to $L_2$ | Existence of poly-time $f$ preserving membership | | $f : \Sigma^* \rightarrow \Sigma^*$ | Reduction function | Polynomial-time computable transformation | | $x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2$ | Equivalence condition | Yes/no answer preserved exactly | | $\vert f(x) \vert \le p(\vert x \vert)$ | Output size bound | Implicit in poly-time computability | | Transitivity | $A \le_p B \wedge B \le_p C \implies A \le_p C$ | Enables chaining of reductions | | Closure property | $\mathcal{P}$ is closed under $\le_p$ | Hard problems can't reduce into $\mathcal{P}$ from outside | | $\mathcal{NP}$-hardness | $L \in \mathcal{NP}\text{-hard} \iff \forall L' \in \mathcal{NP},\ L' \le_p L$ | Definition via universal reduction | | $\mathcal{NP}$-completeness | $L \in \mathcal{NP} \wedge L \in \mathcal{NP}\text{-hard}$ | Decision problems "hardest" in $\mathcal{NP}$ | | Cook-Levin Theorem | $\text{SAT} \in \mathcal{NP}\text{-complete}$ | Foundation theorem for all $\mathcal{NP}$-completeness | | Karp reduction | Many-one poly-time for decision problems | Named after Richard Karp (1972) | > [!NOTE] > **Key Property — Closure:** If $L_2 \in \mathcal{C}$ for any complexity class $\mathcal{C}$ closed under $\le_p$, and $L_1 \le_p L_2$, then $L_1 \in \mathcal{C}$. Classes $\mathcal{P}$, $\mathcal{NP}$, PSPACE, EXPTIME are all closed under polynomial-time many-one reductions. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Polynomial-time reductions underpin several practical domains: - **Cryptanalysis:** Reducing a candidate cipher-breaking problem to a known $\mathcal{NP}$-hard problem (e.g., SAT) is the basis for *security reduction* proofs in cryptographic protocol design. - **Automated Verification:** Model checking of hardware/software systems reduces to SAT or QBF (Quantified Boolean Formula) instances. - **Bioinformatics:** Sequence alignment problems are reduced to shortest-path or flow-network problems in polynomial time. - **Operations Research:** Integer programming is $\mathcal{NP}$-complete; reductions from scheduling, routing, and bin-packing problems justify the need for approximation algorithms. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Theorem: Polynomial-Time Reductions are Transitive **Claim:** If $A \le_p B$ and $B \le_p C$, then $A \le_p C$. ### Proof **Step 1 — Setup of the reductions:** By definition, there exist polynomial-time computable functions $f$ and $g$ and polynomials $p_1, p_2$ such that: $$ f : \Sigma^* \rightarrow \Sigma^*, \quad x \in A \iff f(x) \in B, \quad f \text{ computable in time } p_1(\vert x \vert) $$ $$ g : \Sigma^* \rightarrow \Sigma^*, \quad y \in B \iff g(y) \in C, \quad g \text{ computable in time } p_2(\vert y \vert) $$ **Step 2 — Construct the composition:** Define the composed function $h = g \circ f$, i.e., $h(x) = g(f(x))$. **Step 3 — Verify polynomial-time computability of $h$:** To compute $h(x)$, the reduction algorithm first runs $f$ on $x$ in $p_1(\vert x \vert)$ steps, producing $f(x)$ with output length at most $p_1(\vert x \vert)$. Then it runs $g$ on $f(x)$ in $p_2(\vert f(x) \vert)$ steps. Since $\vert f(x) \vert \le p_1(\vert x \vert)$: $$ \text{Total time}(h) \le p_1(\vert x \vert) + p_2(p_1(\vert x \vert)) $$ Let $P(n) = p_1(n) + p_2(p_1(n))$. Composition of polynomials is a polynomial, so $P(n)$ is a polynomial. Therefore $h$ is computable in polynomial time. **Step 4 — Verify membership preservation:** $$ \begin{aligned} x \in A &\iff f(x) \in B \quad \text{(by definition of } f \text{)} \\ &\iff g(f(x)) \in C \quad \text{(by definition of } g \text{)} \\ &\iff h(x) \in C \quad \text{(by definition of } h \text{)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion:** Since $h$ is polynomial-time computable and preserves membership, we conclude: $$ A \le_p C \quad \blacksquare $$ --- ## 3.2 Theorem: $\mathcal{P}$ is Closed Under Polynomial-Time Reductions **Claim:** If $L_2 \in \mathcal{P}$ and $L_1 \le_p L_2$, then $L_1 \in \mathcal{P}$. ### Proof **Step 1 — Decision algorithm for $L_2$:** Since $L_2 \in \mathcal{P}$, there exists a DTM $M_2$ deciding $L_2$ in time $q(n)$ for some polynomial $q$. **Step 2 — Construction of decision algorithm for $L_1$:** Given $x \in \Sigma^*$, the algorithm for $L_1$ proceeds as follows: 1. Compute $y = f(x)$ using the reduction in time $p(\vert x \vert)$. 2. Simulate $M_2$ on $y$ for $q(\vert y \vert)$ steps. 3.
