Output the same answer as $M_2$. **Step 3 — Bound the total runtime:** Since $\vert y \vert \le p(\vert x \vert)$, the total runtime is bounded by: $$ T(n) \le p(n) + q(p(n)) $$ This is a polynomial in $n$. Hence $L_1$ is decidable in polynomial time, so $L_1 \in \mathcal{P}$. **Step 4 — Equivalence preservation:** By the definition of polynomial-time reduction: $$ x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2 \iff M_2(f(x)) \text{ accepts} \iff \text{our algorithm accepts} $$ Therefore $L_1$ is correctly decided, completing the proof. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Reduction Variants | Property | Many-One ($\le_m^p$) | Turing ($\le_T^p$) | Truth-Table ($\le_{tt}^p$) | |---|---|---|---| | Query model | Single instance $f(x)$ | Multiple adaptive queries | Pre-computed query list | | Power | Weakest | Strongest | Intermediate | | Used for | $\mathcal{NP}$-completeness | Function problems, counting | Bounded query classes | | Transitivity | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Output of $f$ | Single string | Oracle access protocol | Truth-table + index | | KTU weight | **High (most exam questions)** | Medium | Low (mentioned only) | --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — Reduction from 3SAT to Independent Set A canonical reduction that KTU examiners frequently test: **Source Problem (3SAT):** Given a Boolean formula $\phi$ in CNF with each clause having exactly 3 literals, decide whether $\phi$ is satisfiable. **Target Problem (INDEPENDENT-SET):** Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $k$, decide whether $G$ contains an independent set of size $\ge k$. **Reduction Function $f$:** Given $\phi$ with clauses $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m$: 1. For each clause $C_j = (\ell_{j,1} \vee \ell_{j,2} \vee \ell_{j,3})$, create a triangle of three vertices, each labeled with one literal. 2. Add an edge between vertex $u$ (labeled $\ell$) and vertex $v$ (labeled $\ell'$) **iff** $\ell$ and $\ell'$ are in different triangles **and** $\ell = \neg \ell'$ (complementary literals). 3. Set $k = m$ (the number of clauses). **Equivalence Argument:** - **($\Rightarrow$)** If $\phi$ is satisfiable with assignment $\alpha$, select from each triangle the vertex whose literal is set to TRUE by $\alpha$. By complementary-edge construction, no two selected vertices share an edge, yielding an independent set of size $m$. - **($\Leftarrow$)** If $G$ has an independent set of size $m$, it must contain exactly one vertex from each triangle (since any triangle has 3 mutually adjacent vertices, only one can be chosen). Assign TRUE to each selected literal; by the complementary edges, no contradictions arise, so $\phi$ is satisfiable. **Polynomial-Time:** Construction is clearly $\mathcal{O}(\vert \phi \vert)$. Therefore $\text{3SAT} \le_p \text{INDEPENDENT-SET}$. $\blacksquare$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture — Polynomial-Time Reduction Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph srcZone["SOURCE PROBLEM L1"] instX["Instance x"] class1["Decision: x in L1 ?"] end subgraph redZone["REDUCTION MACHINE"] polyTime["Polynomial-Time DTM M"] funcF["f : Sigma* --> Sigma*"] boundStep["p of |x| steps"] end subgraph tgtZone["TARGET PROBLEM L2"] instY["Instance y = f of x"] class2["Decision: y in L2 ?"] end instX --> polyTime polyTime --> funcF polyTime -.controls.-> boundStep funcF --> instY instY --> class2 class1 -.x in L1 iff y in L2.-> class2 ``` ## 4.2 Logical Force Diagram — Hardness Propagation ```mermaid flowchart TB knownHard["Known Hard Problem
e.g., SAT"] candidateNew["Candidate Problem
e.g., CLIQUE"] reductFn["Polynomial-Time
Reduction f"] propArrow["If SAT le_p CLIQUE
then CLIQUE is hard"] closedClass["P closed under le_p
So CLIQUE not in P"] knownHard -->|reduce| reductFn reductFn -->|produces| candidateNew candidateNew --> propArrow propArrow --> closedClass ``` ## 4.3 Transitivity Chain Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Problem A"] B["Problem B"] C["Problem C"] D["Problem D"] A -->|"f1 poly-time"| B B -->|"f2 poly-time"| C C -->|"f3 poly-time"| D note1["f = f3 o f2 o f1
still polynomial-time"] A -.-> note1 note1 -.-> D ``` ## 4.4 Reduction Type Hierarchy (Power Ordering) ```mermaid flowchart TB top["Turing Reduction le_T^p
MOST POWERFUL
multiple oracle queries"] mid["Truth-Table Reduction le_tt^p
precomputed query set"] bot["Many-One Reduction le_m^p
LEAST POWERFUL
single output instance"] top -->|"restricts"| mid mid -->|"restricts"| bot note["Every many-one reduction is also
a truth-table reduction which is also
a Turing reduction."] top -.-> note ``` ## 4.5 Worked Reduction — 3SAT to Independent Set (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph formula["FORMULA phi"] c1["Clause C1
x1 OR not x2 OR x3"] c2["Clause C2
not x1 OR x2 OR x3"] c3["Clause C3
x1 OR x2 OR not x3"] end subgraph graphG["GRAPH G of INDEPENDENT-SET"] t1["Triangle for C1
nodes x1, not x2, x3"] t2["Triangle for C2
nodes not x1, x2, x3"] t3["Triangle for C3
nodes x1, x2, not x3"] end c1 --> t1 c2 --> t2 c3 --> t3 cross1["Cross edges between
complementary literals"] cross2["e.g., x1 in T1
not x1 in T2
edge between them"] t1 -.-> cross1 t2 -.-> cross1 cross1 -.-> cross2 t3 -.-> cross1 kMark["k = 3 clauses
= number of triangles"] graphG --> kMark ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Define polynomial-time reduction. State and explain the transitive property of polynomial-time reductions.** **Model Answer:** A polynomial-time reduction from language $L_1$ to language $L_2$ (written $L_1 \le_p L_2$) is a polynomial-time computable function $f : \Sigma^* \rightarrow \Sigma^*$ such that for every $x \in \Sigma^*$: $$ x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2 $$ **Transitivity:** If $L_1 \le_p L_2$ and $L_2 \le_p L_3$, then $L_1 \le_p L_3$. **Proof Sketch:** Let $f$ be the reduction from $L_1$ to $L_2$ (computable in time $p_1$), and $g$ be the reduction from $L_2$ to $L_3$ (computable in time $p_2$). Define $h(x) = g(f(x))$. The function $h$ is computable in time $p_1(n) + p_2(p_1(n))$, which is polynomial. Also, $x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2 \iff g(f(x)) \in L_3 \iff h(x) \in L_3$. Hence $L_1 \le_p L_3$. $\blacksquare$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to argue **why** $h$ is polynomial-time. You must explicitly state that composition of polynomials is a polynomial, so the runtime bound is still valid. **(−1 mark deduction if skipped)** --- ### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Explain why polynomial-time reductions are used (instead of, say, logarithmic-space or exponential-time reductions) for defining $\mathcal{NP}$-completeness.** **Model Answer:** Polynomial-time reductions are the **standard** for $\mathcal{NP}$-completeness for three deep reasons: 1. **Tractability Preservation:** If $L_1 \le_p L_2$ and $L_2 \in \mathcal{P}$, then $L_1 \in \mathcal{P}$. This makes the reduction "useful" — a polynomial-time algorithm for the target solves the source in polynomial time. 2. **Robustness:** By the **Cobham-Edmonds Thesis**, polynomial time is invariant across all reasonable deterministic computational models (single-tape, multi-tape, RAM machines). This makes $\mathcal{P}$ and the reductions machine-independent. 3. **Closure of $\mathcal{NP}$:** $\mathcal{NP}$ is closed under polynomial-time reductions. Hence the class of problems reducible to SAT (the canonical hard problem) sits naturally inside $\mathcal{NP}$. Logarithmic-space reductions are stricter and used for $\mathcal{P}$-completeness. Exponential-time reductions would be too weak to capture meaningful hardness relationships. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse the **"polynomial-time reduction"** used for $\mathcal{NP}$-completeness with the **"log-space reduction"** used for $\mathcal{P}$-completeness. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. **(−2 marks if confused)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question Choice A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] #### (a) [7 Marks] Prove that if $L_1 \le_p L_2$ and $L_2 \in \mathcal{P}$, then $L_1 \in \mathcal{P}$. Discuss the significance of this theorem in complexity theory. **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Setup:** Given $L_1 \le_p L_2$, there exists a polynomial-time DTM $M_f$ computing $f$ in time $p_1(n)$. Since $L_2 \in \mathcal{P}$, there exists a DTM $M_2$ deciding $L_2$ in time $p_2(n)$. **Step 2 — Construction of $M_1$:** Construct a DTM $M_1$ that decides $L_1$: 1. On input $x$, simulate $M_f$ to compute $y = f(x)$. 2. Simulate $M_2$ on input $y$. 3. If $M_2$ accepts, accept; else reject. **Step 3 — Runtime bound:** $$ T_{M_1}(n) \le p_1(n) + p_2(p_1(n)) = \mathcal{O}(p(n)) \text{ for some polynomial } p $$ Hence $M_1$ runs in polynomial time. **Step 4 — Correctness:** $$ x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2 \iff M_2(f(x)) \text{ accepts} \iff M_1 \text{ accepts} $$ **Step 5 — Conclusion:** $M_1$ is a polynomial-time decider for $L_1$, so $L_1 \in \mathcal{P}$. $\blacksquare$ **Significance in Complexity Theory:** - This theorem is the **foundation of all hardness-preservation proofs**. - Its **contrapositive** ($L_1 \notin \mathcal{P} \wedge L_1 \le_p L_2 \implies L_2 \notin \mathcal{P}$) is used to prove that problems are **not** in $\mathcal{P}$ — assuming $\mathcal{P} \ne \mathcal{NP}$. - It justifies the entire $\mathcal{NP}$-completeness framework: if $\mathcal{P} = \mathcal{NP}$, then **every** $\mathcal{NP}$-complete problem would be in $\mathcal{P}$. #### (b) [7 Marks] Reduce the problem VERTEX-COVER to SET-COVER formally and prove the correctness of the reduction. **Problem Statements:** - **VERTEX-COVER:** Given graph $G = (V, E)$ and integer $k$, does there exist a vertex cover of size $\le k$? (A vertex cover is a subset $V' \subseteq V$ such that every edge has at least one endpoint in $V'$.) - **SET-COVER:** Given a universe $U$, a collection $\mathcal{S} = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_m\}$ where $S_i \subseteq U$, and integer $k$, does there exist a sub-collection of at most $k$ sets that covers $U$? **Reduction Function $f$:** Given instance $(G, k)$ of VERTEX-COVER: 1. Set the universe $U = E$ (the set of edges). 2. For each vertex $v \in V$, create a set $S_v = \{ e \in E : v \text{ is incident to } e \}$. 3. Let $\mathcal{S} = \{ S_v : v \in V \}$. 4. Output the instance $(U, \mathcal{S}, k)$ of SET-COVER. **Polynomial Runtime:** Construction runs in $\mathcal{O}(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert) = \mathcal{O}(\vert G \vert)$ time, clearly polynomial. **Correctness Proof:** **($\Rightarrow$)** Suppose $V' \subseteq V$ is a vertex cover of $G$ with $\vert V' \vert \le k$. Consider the corresponding set family $\{S_v : v \in V'\}$. For any edge $e = (u, w) \in E$, since $V'$ covers every edge, either $u \in V'$ or $w \in V'$. Hence $e \in S_u$ or $e \in S_w$. Thus the family $\{S_v : v \in V'\}$ covers $U = E$, and has size $\le k$. So SET-COVER has a solution. **($\Leftarrow$)** Suppose $\{S_{v_1}, S_{v_2}, \ldots, S_{v_j}\}$ with $j \le k$ covers $U = E$. This means every edge $e \in E$ belongs to some $S_{v_i}$, meaning $e$ is incident to $v_i$. Thus $\{v_1, \ldots, v_j\}$ is a vertex cover of $G$ with size $\le k$. **Conclusion:** $\text{VERTEX-COVER} \le_p \text{SET-COVER}$. $\blacksquare$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - Many students **omit the explicit construction of the sets** $S_v$. You must write $S_v = \{e \in E : v \text{ incident to } e\}$. **(−2 marks)** > - Both directions ($\Rightarrow$ and $\Leftarrow$) of the equivalence must be proven; one direction alone loses 3 marks. > - Stating the runtime bound is mandatory even if "obviously polynomial." **(−1 mark)** --- ### Question Choice B [KTU University Exam — June 2023] #### (a) [7 Marks] Define $\mathcal{NP}$-completeness via polynomial-time reductions. Explain the Cook-Levin Theorem and its importance. **Model Answer:** **Definition:** A language $L$ is $\mathcal{NP}$-**complete** if: 1. $L \in \mathcal{NP}$, and 2. For every language $L' \in \mathcal{NP}$, we have $L' \le_p L$ (i.e., $L$ is $\mathcal{NP}$-hard). **Cook-Levin Theorem (1971):** $\text{SAT}$ (Boolean Satisfiability) is $\mathcal{NP}$-complete. **Proof Outline:** **Step 1 — SAT $\in \mathcal{NP}$:** Given a Boolean formula $\phi$ and an assignment $\alpha$, verify $\phi(\alpha) = \text{TRUE}$ in polynomial time. Hence $\text{SAT} \in \mathcal{NP}$. **Step 2 — SAT is $\mathcal{NP}$-hard:** Let $L \in \mathcal{NP}$ be arbitrary. There exists a nondeterministic polynomial-time Turing machine $N$ deciding $L$. Construct a polynomial-time reduction $f$ that, given $x$, builds a Boolean formula $\phi_x$ such that: $$ \phi_x \text{ is satisfiable} \iff N \text{ accepts } x $$ The construction uses **configuration graphs**: each node represents a global configuration of $N$, and clauses encode valid transitions of $N$. The formula has size polynomial in $\vert x \vert$. **Importance of Cook-Levin Theorem:** - It is the **"anchor"** of $\mathcal{NP}$-completeness theory. Without it, the entire concept would be empty. - It enables the use of **Karp's 21 reductions** (1972): once SAT is shown $\mathcal{NP}$-complete, **thousands** of other problems become $\mathcal{NP}$-complete via transitive reductions from SAT. - It underlies the **$\mathcal{P}$ vs $\mathcal{NP}$** Millennium Prize Problem. #### (b) [7 Marks] Reduce the HAMILTONIAN-CYCLE problem to the TRAVELING-SALESMAN-PROBLEM (TSP). Prove the reduction is correct. **Problem Statements:** - **HAMILTONIAN-CYCLE (HC):** Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with $\vert V \vert = n$, does $G$ contain a cycle visiting each vertex exactly once? - **TSP-DECISION:** Given $n$ cities with pairwise distances $d(i, j)$ and integer $B$, is there a tour of total length $\le B$? **Reduction Function $f$:** Given graph $G = (V, E)$ with $V = \{v_1, \ldots, v_n\}$: 1. Define $n$ cities corresponding to $V$. 2. Define the distance matrix: $$ d(v_i, v_j) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \{v_i, v_j\} \in E \\ 2 & \text{if } \{v_i, v_j\} \notin E \end{cases} $$ 3. Set bound $B = n$. Output the TSP instance with these distances and bound $B$. **Polynomial Runtime:** Construction takes $\mathcal{O}(n^2)$ time to encode all pairwise distances. **Correctness Proof:** **($\Rightarrow$)** Suppose $G$ has a Hamiltonian cycle $C = (v_{i_1}, v_{i_2}, \ldots, v_{i_n}, v_{i_1})$. Every edge of $C$ is in $E$, so every distance along $C$ is exactly $1$. Total tour length $= n \cdot 1 = n = B$. Hence the TSP instance has a tour of length $\le B$. **($\Leftarrow$)** Suppose the TSP instance has a tour of length $\le n$ visiting all cities exactly once. The tour has $n$ edges. Each edge contributes either $1$ or $2$ to the length. If any edge contributes $2$, the total length would be $\ge 2 + (n-1) \cdot 1 = n + 1 > n$, contradiction. So every edge must contribute $1$, meaning every pair of consecutive cities in the tour is connected in $G$. Hence the tour defines a Hamiltonian cycle in $G$. **Conclusion:** $\text{HAMILTONIAN-CYCLE} \le_p \text{TSP}$. $\blacksquare$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - Many students set $B = n-1$ or $B = n+1$ — **incorrect bounds**. The correct bound is $B = n$. > - Students often skip the **edge weight assignment case analysis** in the $\Leftarrow$ direction. You must argue why every edge must be weight $1$. **(−3 marks)** > - Failing to verify polynomial-time computability of $f$. **(−1 mark)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Polynomial-Time Reductions** - **Definition Core:** $L_1 \le_p L_2$ iff there exists a polynomial-time computable $f$ with $x \in L_1 \iff f(x) \in L_2$. - **Three reduction types in KTU scope:** Many-one (most common), Turing, Truth-table. - **Transitivity:** $A \le_p B \wedge B \le_p C \implies A \le_p C$. Composition of polynomial-time functions is polynomial-time. - **Closure under $\le_p$:** Classes $\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{NP}$, PSPACE, EXPTIME are all closed under polynomial-time many-one reductions. - **Hardness propagation (contrapositive):** $L_1 \notin \mathcal{P} \wedge L_1 \le_p L_2 \implies L_2 \notin \mathcal{P}$ — this is the workhorse for proving problems are $\mathcal{NP}$-hard. - **Cook-Levin Theorem (1971):** SAT is $\mathcal{NP}$-complete — the foundational result. - **Karp's 21 Problems (1972):** The first batch of $\mathcal{NP}$-complete problems, all derived from SAT via polynomial-time reductions. - **Canonical reductions to memorize:** 3SAT $\to$ INDEPENDENT-SET, 3SAT $\to$ VERTEX-COVER, VERTEX-COVER $\to$ SET-COVER, HAMILTONIAN-CYCLE $\to$ TSP. - **Output size bound:** $\vert f(x) \vert \le p(\vert x \vert)$ — implicit but must be acknowledged. - **Yields-vs-answers:** Reductions preserve **decision** answers, not optimization values directly. - **$\mathcal{P}$ vs $\mathcal{NP}$ link:** If any $\mathcal{NP}$-complete problem is in $\mathcal{P}$, then $\mathcal{P} = \mathcal{NP}$. - **Exam-style mark breakdown:** A typical 14-mark reduction question awards 2 marks for formal setup, 4 marks for construction of $f$, 4 marks for $\Rightarrow$ proof, 3 marks for $\Leftarrow$ proof, and 1 mark for the polynomial-time bound.

topics.title: NP-Complete Problems, Their Significance, and Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECST864 - COMPUTATIONAL COMPLEXITY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** NP-Complete Problems, Their Significance, and Examples # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of NP-Completeness > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board-Exam Critical):** > A decision problem $\Pi$ is said to be **NP-Complete** if and only if it satisfies the following two conditions simultaneously: > > 1. **Membership in NP:** $\Pi \in \mathbf{NP}$ (i.e., every "YES" instance has a certificate that can be verified in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine). > 2. **NP-Hardness:** For every language $L' \in \mathbf{NP}$, there exists a polynomial-time many-to-one reduction $L' \leq_{p} \Pi$. Equivalently, $\Pi$ is at least as hard as **every** problem in the class NP. Mathematically, the class of NP-Complete problems is defined as: $$\mathbf{NPC} \;=\; \mathbf{NP} \;\cap\; \mathbf{NP\text{-Hard}}$$ The class $\mathbf{NP\text{-Hard}}$ is the set of problems $H$ such that for every $L \in \mathbf{NP}$, $L \leq_{p} H$. The intersection with $\mathbf{NP}$ restricts NP-Hardness to problems that are *themselves* verifiable in polynomial time. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Exam Hall Detective" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy for First-Time Learners:** > > Imagine you walk into an exam hall and the invigilator hands you a sealed envelope marked **"The Hardest Question Ever."** You are told two things: > - **(Verification Property)** — If someone whispers the *answer* to you, you can check it within a few minutes (i.e., polynomial time). > - **(Reduction Property)** — Every other "hard" question from every other subject in the entire university can be *converted*, with a small overhead, into a copy of *this exact* question. > > That envelope is an **NP-Complete** problem. It is "complete" not because it is the only hard problem, but because the *entire* NP universe can be funneled into it. In engineering terms: NP-Complete problems form the **hardest kernel of verifiable problems**. Cracking one in polynomial time cracks them all; failing to crack one (the standard assumption, $\mathbf{P} \neq \mathbf{NP}$) preserves the boundary of efficient computation. ## 1.3 Why the Concept Exists in the KTU Syllabus > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this topic matters for the 2024 Scheme:** > NP-Completeness is the theoretical foundation that allows computer scientists and software engineers to **classify** computational problems into "solvable in practice" versus "inherently intractable." It guides real-world decisions in: > - **Algorithm design** — knowing when to abandon exact solutions and switch to approximations, heuristics, or randomized algorithms. > - **Cryptography** — many encryption schemes (e.g., RSA) rely on the presumed hardness of NP-Complete problems like integer factorization. > - **Operations Research** — scheduling, routing, and resource allocation problems (TSP, Knapsack) are NP-Complete, so optimality guarantees are replaced by approximation ratios. > - **Hardware/compiler optimization** — register allocation, instruction scheduling, and Boolean satisfiability are NP-Complete, motivating SAT solvers and heuristic engines. The **Cook-Levin Theorem (1971)** is the cornerstone: it established that the **Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT)** is NP-Complete, giving us the first "seed" problem from which all others were derived by reduction. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Proving a Problem is NP-Complete A standard KTU/Karp-style proof that a problem $\Pi$ is NP-Complete proceeds in three structured steps: ### Pillar 1 — Show $\Pi \in \mathbf{NP}$ - Construct a deterministic polynomial-time verifier $V$. - Given an instance $x$ and a certificate $c$, $V$ decides in time $O(n^{k})$ whether $c$ is a valid witness for $x$. - *Example for SAT:* Given a Boolean formula $\phi$ and a truth assignment $c$, evaluate $\phi(c)$ in $O(n)$ time. ### Pillar 2 — Choose a Known NP-Complete Problem $\Pi'$ - Standard reference problems include SAT, 3-SAT, Vertex Cover, Hamiltonian Cycle, etc. - The choice is strategic — pick $\Pi'$ whose structure *resembles* $\Pi$. ### Pillar 3 — Construct a Polynomial-Time Reduction $\Pi' \leq_{p} \Pi$ - Design a transformation function $f$ such that: $$x \in \Pi' \iff f(x) \in \Pi$$ - The transformation $f$ must run in time $O(n^{k})$ for some constant $k$. - This ensures that any polynomial algorithm for $\Pi$ would, in turn, solve $\Pi'$ in polynomial time. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** Once a single NP-Complete problem is established (Cook-Levin gave us SAT), every subsequent NP-Complete problem requires only a polynomial reduction — not a re-proof from scratch. This is why there are now **thousands** of known NP-Complete problems. ## 2.2 The Reduction Closure Property The set $\mathbf{NPC}$ is **closed under polynomial-time many-to-one reductions**: $$\text{If } L_{1} \in \mathbf{NPC},\ L_{2} \in \mathbf{NP},\ \text{and } L_{1} \leq_{p} L_{2}, \ \text{then } L_{2} \in \mathbf{NPC}.$$ This transitive-style closure is what makes the "ladder of reductions" possible: SAT $\to$ 3-SAT $\to$ Independent Set $\to$ Clique $\to$ Vertex Cover $\to$ … and so on. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Reference Table — Memorize for ESE** | Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Notes / Units | |---------------|----------------------|---------------| | $\mathbf{P}$ | Class solvable in deterministic polynomial time $O(n^{k})$ | "Efficient" / "Tractable" | | $\mathbf{NP}$ | Class with polynomial-time verifiable certificates | Non-deterministic Polynomial | | $\mathbf{NP\text{-Hard}}$ | $\{ H \mid \forall L \in \mathbf{NP},\ L \leq_{p} H \}$ | At least as hard as NP | | $\mathbf{NPC}$ | $\mathbf{NP} \cap \mathbf{NP\text{-Hard}}$ | The NP-Complete class | | $\leq_{p}$ | Polynomial-time many-toone reduction | "Karp reduction" | | $\leq_{T}^{p}$ | Polynomial-time Turing reduction | "Cook reduction" | | Cook-Levin | $\text{SAT} \in \mathbf{NPC}$ | First NP-Complete problem (1971) | | Karp's 21 | 21 NP-Complete problems | Reduction catalog (1972) | | $O(n^{k})$ | Polynomial time bound | $k$ is a fixed constant | | $\mathbf{P} \subseteq \mathbf{NP}$ | Trivially true | Open: is equality strict? | | $\mathbf{NP} \subseteq \mathbf{PSPACE}$ | True | NPSPACE = PSPACE | | $\mathbf{NPC} \subseteq \mathbf{co\text{-}NP}$ | Open | Equals NP if NP = co-NP | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | NP-Complete Subproblem | Practical Consequence | |--------------------|-------------------------|------------------------| | VLSI Design | Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) | Modern SAT solvers (z3, MiniSAT) use heuristics | | Compilers | Register allocation, instruction scheduling | Graph coloring heuristic approaches | | Bioinformatics | Protein folding, sequence alignment | Heuristics, dynamic programming approximations | | Logistics | Vehicle routing, job-shop scheduling | ILP solvers + approximation algorithms | | Network Security | Public-key cryptography | Factoring-based schemes rely on presumed hardness | | AI / Planning | Planning, propositional logic reasoning | Bounded suboptimality via weighted A* | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Takeaway:** The presence of NP-Completeness does **not** mean "give up." It means: *replace exact optimality with bounded approximation*, *replace worst-case with average-case heuristics*, or *replace deterministic with randomized/quantum* strategies. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Reductions ## 3.1 Derivation: The Decision Version of a Classic NP-Complete Problem To give the derivation rigour expected at KTU valuation, we work through the formalization of the **Subset Sum Problem**, which is a benchmark NP-Complete problem. ### 3.1.1 Formal Statement of SUBSET-SUM > **SUBSET-SUM (Decision Form):** > *Instance:* A finite set $S = \{a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{n}\}$ of positive integers and a target integer $t$. > *Question:* Does there exist a subset $S' \subseteq S$ such that > $$\sum_{a_{i} \in S'} a_{i} \;=\; t ?$$ ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Proof that SUBSET-SUM is NP-Complete **Step 1 — Show SUBSET-SUM $\in$ NP.** Given a candidate subset $S' \subseteq S$, the verifier performs the following: 1. Check that $\vert S' \vert \leq n$ (size check). 2. Compute the sum $\sigma = \sum_{a_{i} \in S'} a_{i}$ by iterating over the elements of $S'$. 3. Accept if and only if $\sigma = t$. The summation requires at most $O(n)$ additions, each on integers of size at most $O(n \log n)$ bits. Hence the verification runs in time polynomial in the input size. Therefore, SUBSET-SUM $\in$ NP. **Step 2 — Reduce a known NP-Complete problem to SUBSET-SUM.** We reduce from **3-SAT** (which is NP-Complete by Cook-Levin + Karp reduction). Given a 3-CNF formula $\phi = C_{1} \wedge C_{2} \wedge \cdots \wedge C_{m}$ over variables $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n}$, we construct an instance $(S, t)$ of SUBSET-SUM as follows: $$\text{For each variable } x_{i}, \text{ create two integers encoded in a positional base } B = (n+1):$$ $$a_{i} \;\mapsto\; (B^{i}) \quad \text{(representing } x_{i} = \text{TRUE})$$ $$\bar{a}_{i} \;\mapsto\; (B^{i}) \quad \text{(representing } x_{i} = \text{FALSE})$$ For each clause $C_{j} = (\ell_{j,1} \vee \ell_{j,2} \vee \ell_{j,3})$, we add three "slack" integers with values $B^{n+j}$, one for each literal. These allow the reduction to compensate for at most one literal per clause being excluded. The target sum is: $$t \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} B^{i} \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{m} 2 \cdot B^{n+j}$$ This construction is polynomial because we create at most $2n + 3m$ integers, each of bit-size $O(n \log n)$. The base encoding ensures no carries propagate between positions, so digit-wise analysis is sufficient. **Step 3 — Conclude the proof.** By the standard argument (digit-by-digit), a subset of $S$ sums to $t$ if and only if for each variable position $i$ we pick exactly one of $a_{i}$ or $\bar{a}_{i}$ (fixing the truth assignment), and for each clause position $n+j$ we achieve the digit value $2$ (meaning at least one of the clause's literals is TRUE, i.e., the clause is satisfied). Hence: $$\phi \in \text{3-SAT} \iff (S, t) \in \text{SUBSET-SUM}$$ Therefore, 3-SAT $\leq_{p}$ SUBSET-SUM, and by the closure property, SUBSET-SUM is NP-Complete. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Verifying a Subset Sum Instance > **Example Instance (KTU-style):** > Let $S = \{3, 7, 11, 14\}$ and target $t = 21$. **Solution walkthrough:** Try subsets: - $\{3, 7, 11\} = 21$ ✓ - Hence the answer is **YES**, with certificate $S' = \{3, 7, 11\}$. The verifier would: 1. Receive the certificate $c = \{3, 7, 11\}$. 2. Compute $3 + 7 + 11 = 21$. 3. Compare to $t = 21$. Match — accept. Verification time: $O(n)$ additions = $O(4)$ — polynomial. ## 3.3 Mapping of Common NP-Complete Problems (Karp's Catalog) | # | Problem | Formal Statement (Decision Form) | Reduced From | KTU Priority | |---|---------|--------------------------------|---------------|---------------| | 1 | SAT | Is a given Boolean formula satisfiable? | (Cook-Levin) | High | | 2 | 3-SAT | Same, but each clause has exactly 3 literals | SAT | High | | 3 | CLIQUE | Does a graph $G$ have a clique of size $\geq k$? | 3-SAT | High | | 4 | INDEPENDENT SET | Does $G$ have an independent set of size $\geq k$? | CLIQUE | High | | 5 | VERTEX COVER | Does $G$ have a vertex cover of size $\leq k$? | INDEPENDENT SET | High | | 6 | HAMILTONIAN CYCLE | Does $G$ contain a Hamiltonian cycle? | 3-SAT | High | | 7 | TSP (Decision) | Is there a tour of cost $\leq k$? | HAMILTONIAN CYCLE | High | | 8 | GRAPH COLORING | Is $G$ $k$-colorable? | 3-SAT | Medium | | 9 | SUBSET SUM | See Section 3.1.1 | 3-SAT | High | | 10 | PARTITION | Can $S$ be partitioned into two equal-sum subsets? | SUBSET SUM | Medium | | 11 | KNAPSACK (Decision) | Is there a subset with value $\geq V$ and weight $\leq W$? | SUBSET SUM | Medium | | 12 | INTEGER PROGRAMMING | Is there an integer solution to $Ax \leq b$? | 3-SAT | Low | | 13 | SET COVER | Is there a cover of size $\leq k$? | VERTEX COVER | Medium | | 14 | DOMINATING SET | Is there a dominating set of size $\leq k$? | VERTEX COVER | Medium | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Complexity Class Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD P["P Class
Deterministic Polynomial"] NP["NP Class
Nondeterministic Polynomial"] CoNP["co-NP Class
Complement of NP"] NPC["NP-Complete
NPC = NP intersection NP-Hard"] NPHard["NP-Hard Class
At least as hard as NP"] PSPACE["PSPACE Class
Polynomial Space"] EXPTIME["EXPTIME Class
Exponential Time"] SAT["SAT Problem
First NP-Complete"] ThreeSAT["3-SAT Problem"] VC["Vertex Cover"] HC["Hamiltonian Cycle"] CLIQUE["Clique"] SS["Subset Sum"] P -->|"subset of"| NP NP -->|"subset of"| PSPACE CoNP -->|"subset of"| PSPACE NP -->|"equals if P=NP"| P NPHard -->|"contains"| NPC NP -->|"intersects"| NPC PSPACE -->|"subset of"| EXPTIME SAT -->|"Karp reduced to"| ThreeSAT ThreeSAT -->|"reduced to"| VC ThreeSAT -->|"reduced to"| HC ThreeSAT -->|"reduced to"| CLIQUE CLIQUE -->|"complement"| VC ThreeSAT -->|"reduced to"| SS NPC -->|"examples"| SAT NPC -->|"examples"| ThreeSAT NPC -->|"examples"| VC NPC -->|"examples"| HC NPC -->|"examples"| CLIQUE NPC -->|"examples"| SS style P fill:#90EE90 style NP fill:#FFD700 style CoNP fill:#FFD700 style NPC fill:#FF6347 style NPHard fill:#DC143C style PSPACE fill:#87CEEB style EXPTIME fill:#DDA0DD style SAT fill:#FFA07A style ThreeSAT fill:#FFA07A style VC fill:#FFA07A style HC fill:#FFA07A style CLIQUE fill:#FFA07A style SS fill:#FFA07A ``` ## 4.2 The Reduction Ladder (Karp-Style Network) ```mermaid graph LR SAT1["SAT"] SAT3["3-SAT"] IS["Independent Set"] CL["Clique"] VC["Vertex Cover"] HC["Hamiltonian Cycle"] TSP["Travelling Salesman"] GC["Graph 3-Coloring"] SS2["Subset Sum"] KP["Knapsack"] PC["Partition"] SAT1 -->|"restructure clauses"| SAT3 SAT3 -->|"variable gadget"| IS IS -->|"complement graph"| CL IS -->|"dual problem"| VC SAT3 -->|"cycle gadget"| HC HC -->|"cost encoding"| TSP SAT3 -->|"color gadget"| GC SAT3 -->|"positional encoding"| SS2 SS2 -->|"add constant"| KP SS2 -->|"balance check"| PC style SAT1 fill:#FF6347 style SAT3 fill:#FF6347 style IS fill:#FF6347 style CL fill:#FF6347 style VC fill:#FF6347 style HC fill:#FF6347 style TSP fill:#FF6347 style GC fill:#FF6347 style SS2 fill:#FF6347 style KP fill:#FF6347 style PC fill:#FF6347 ``` ## 4.3 The Three-Pillar NP-Completeness Proof Flow ```mermaid graph TD Start["Start: Problem Π"] P1["Pillar 1: Show Π in NP
Construct polynomial verifier V"] P2["Pillar 2: Pick known NP-Complete Π-prime
e.g. 3-SAT, Vertex Cover"] P3["Pillar 3: Build polynomial reduction f
x in Π-prime iff f(x) in Π"] Verify["Verify: f runs in O of n to the k"] Conclude["Conclusion: Π is NP-Complete"] Fail1["Pillar 1 fails
Π might be NP-Hard only"] Fail2["Pillar 3 fails
Reduction not polynomial"] Start --> P1 P1 -->|"verified"| P2 P1 -->|"fails"| Fail1 P2 --> P3 P3 -->|"verified"| Verify P3 -->|"fails"| Fail2 Verify --> Conclude style Start fill:#87CEEB style P1 fill:#90EE90 style P2 fill:#FFD700 style P3 fill:#FFA500 style Verify fill:#FF6347 style Conclude fill:#DC143C style Fail1 fill:#696969 style Fail2 fill:#696969 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define NP-Completeness. State the Cook-Levin theorem and explain its significance.** **Model Answer:** A decision problem $\Pi$ is **NP-Complete** if: 1. $\Pi \in \mathbf{NP}$ (verifiable in polynomial time), and 2. For every $L \in \mathbf{NP}$, $L \leq_{p} \Pi$ (every NP problem polynomial-reduces to $\Pi$). **Cook-Levin Theorem (1971):** The Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT) is NP-Complete. **Significance:** - It provides the *first* proven NP-Complete problem. - It acts as the "root" of the entire reduction tree — every subsequent NP-Completeness proof reduces from SAT (directly or transitively). - It established the polynomial-time reduction as the standard tool for hardness classification. - It crystallized the $\mathbf{P}$ vs. $\mathbf{NP}$ question as a single concrete open problem. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Distinguish between NP, NP-Hard, and NP-Complete classes with a suitable Venn-diagram-like description.** **Model Answer:** | Class | Definition | Subset Relation | |-------|------------|-----------------| | $\mathbf{NP}$ | Problems with polynomial-time verifiable YES certificates | — | | $\mathbf{NP\text{-Hard}}$ | Problems to which every NP problem polynomial-reduces | May lie outside NP | | $\mathbf{NP\text{-Complete}}$ | $\mathbf{NP} \cap \mathbf{NP\text{-Hard}}$ | Hardest problems *inside* NP | **Hierarchy:** $$\mathbf{NP\text{-Hard}} \supseteq \mathbf{NP\text{-Complete}} \subseteq \mathbf{NP}$$ An example of a problem that is NP-Hard but **not** NP-Complete is the **Halting Problem** (it is undecidable, hence not in NP). SAT, 3-SAT, and CLIQUE are NP-Complete. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of polynomial-time reduction. Show that the **Independent Set** problem polynomial-time reduces to the **Clique** problem. **(b) [7 Marks]** List any five classical NP-Complete problems and state the decision form of each. Briefly describe the reduction chain from SAT to 3-SAT. --- #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Definition (Polynomial-Time Reduction):** A language $L_{1}$ polynomial-time reduces to $L_{2}$, written $L_{1} \leq_{p} L_{2}$, if there exists a deterministic polynomial-time computable function $f : \Sigma^{*} \to \Sigma^{*}$ such that for all $x$: $$x \in L_{1} \iff f(x) \in L_{2}.$$ If such a reduction exists and $L_{2} \in \mathbf{P}$, then $L_{1} \in \mathbf{P}$. **Reduction: Independent Set $\leq_{p}$ Clique** Let $G = (V, E)$ and $k$ be an instance of Independent Set. Construct $f(G, k) = (\bar{G}, k)$ where $\bar{G}$ is the **complement graph** of $G$ with: $$\bar{E} = \{ (u, v) \mid u \neq v \text{ and } (u, v) \notin E \}.$$ **Claim:** $G$ has an independent set of size $\geq k$ if and only if $\bar{G}$ has a clique of size $\geq k$. **Proof of Claim:** - $(\Rightarrow)$ Suppose $S \subseteq V$ is an independent set in $G$ with $\vert S \vert \geq k$. For any $u, v \in S$, $(u, v) \notin E$, so $(u, v) \in \bar{E}$. Hence every pair in $S$ is connected in $\bar{G}$, making $S$ a clique of size $\geq k$ in $\bar{G}$. - $(\Leftarrow)$ Suppose $C \subseteq V$ is a clique in $\bar{G}$ with $\vert C \vert \geq k$. For any $u, v \in C$, $(u, v) \in \bar{E}$, so $(u, v) \notin E$. Hence $C$ is an independent set in $G$ of size $\geq k$. **Polynomial Time:** Computing the complement of a graph takes $O(n^{2})$ time, which is polynomial. Hence Independent Set $\leq_{p}$ Clique. Since Independent Set is NP-Complete (by reduction from 3-SAT), Clique is also NP-Complete. --- #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Five classical NP-Complete problems (Decision Forms):** 1. **SAT:** Given a Boolean formula $\phi$ over variables $\{x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n}\}$, is there an assignment satisfying $\phi$? 2. **3-SAT:** Same as SAT, but $\phi$ is in 3-CNF (each clause has exactly 3 literals). 3. **Vertex Cover:** Given graph $G$ and integer $k$, does $G$ have a vertex cover of size $\leq k$? (A vertex cover is a set $S \subseteq V$ such that every edge has at least one endpoint in $S$.) 4. **Hamiltonian Cycle:** Given graph $G$, does $G$ contain a cycle visiting every vertex exactly once? 5. **Subset Sum:** Given integers $a_{1}, \ldots, a_{n}$ and target $t$, is there a subset summing to exactly $t$? **Reduction Chain from SAT to 3-SAT:** Given a general Boolean formula $\phi$ in CNF with clauses of varying length: 1. Introduce a new auxiliary variable $y_{i}$ for each clause $C_{i} = (\ell_{1} \vee \ell_{2} \vee \cdots \vee \ell_{r})$ where $r > 3$. 2. Replace $C_{i}$ with the chain of clauses: $$(\ell_{1} \vee \ell_{2} \vee y_{1}) \wedge (\bar{y}_{1} \vee \ell_{3} \vee y_{2}) \wedge \cdots \wedge (\bar{y}_{r-2} \vee \ell_{r-1} \vee \ell_{r})$$ 3. This produces an equivalent 3-CNF formula $\phi'$. 4. The transformation is polynomial (linear in the size of $\phi$). 5. Therefore, $\phi$ is satisfiable if and only if $\phi'$ is satisfiable, establishing SAT $\leq_{p}$ 3-SAT. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Define the classes P, NP, and NP-Complete. Prove that if any NP-Complete problem is in P, then P = NP. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Travelling Salesman Problem (Decision version)**. Show its NP-Completeness by reducing from the **Hamiltonian Cycle** problem. --- #### **Model Solution for (a):** **Definitions:** - $\mathbf{P}$: The class of decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in time $O(n^{k})$ for some constant $k$. - $\mathbf{NP}$: The class of decision problems where, for every YES instance $x$, there exists a certificate $c$ of size polynomial in $\vert x \vert$ such that a deterministic verifier can confirm the YES answer in time polynomial in $\vert x \vert$. - $\mathbf{NP\text{-Complete}}$: A problem $\Pi \in \mathbf{NP}$ such that every $L \in \mathbf{NP}$ satisfies $L \leq_{p} \Pi$. **Theorem:** If any NP-Complete problem $\Pi$ belongs to $\mathbf{P}$, then $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{NP}$. **Proof:** Assume $\Pi \in \mathbf{NPC} \cap \mathbf{P}$. Let $L$ be an arbitrary language in $\mathbf{NP}$. We must show $L \in \mathbf{P}$. By NP-Completeness of $\Pi$, there exists a polynomial-time reduction $f$ such that: $$x \in L \iff f(x) \in \Pi.$$ Since $\Pi \in \mathbf{P}$, there is a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm $A_{\Pi}$ deciding $\Pi$ in time $O(n^{k})$ for some constant $k$. Construct an algorithm $A_{L}$ for $L$ as follows: 1. On input $x$, compute $y = f(x)$. The reduction $f$ runs in time $O(n^{c})$ for some constant $c$, and $\vert y \vert \leq n^{c}$ (polynomial growth). 2. Run $A_{\Pi}$ on input $y$. This takes $O((\vert y \vert)^{k}) = O((n^{c})^{k}) = O(n^{ck})$ time. 3. Accept $x$ if and only if $A_{\Pi}$ accepts $y$. **Total running time:** $O(n^{c}) + O(n^{ck}) = O(n^{ck})$ — polynomial in $n$. By the arbitrary choice of $L \in \mathbf{NP}$, every problem in NP is decidable in polynomial time, so $\mathbf{NP} \subseteq \mathbf{P}$. Since $\mathbf{P} \subseteq \mathbf{NP}$ trivially, we conclude $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{NP}$. $\blacksquare$ --- #### **Model Solution for (b):** **Travelling Salesman Problem (Decision Version):** > **TSP-DECISION:** > *Instance:* A complete graph $K_{n}$ with integer edge weights $w : E \to \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$ and a budget $B \in \mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$. > *Question:* Does there exist a Hamiltonian cycle in $K_{n}$ whose total weight is at most $B$? **Reduction: Hamiltonian Cycle $\leq_{p}$ TSP-DECISION** Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ with $\vert V \vert = n$, we construct an instance of TSP-DECISION as follows: - Construct a complete graph $K_{n}$ on the same vertex set $V$. - Define edge weights: $$w(u, v) = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } (u, v) \in E \text{ in } G, \\ 2, & \text{if } (u, v) \notin E \text{ in } G. \end{cases}$$ - Set the budget $B = n$ (the number of edges in a Hamiltonian cycle). **Claim:** $G$ has a Hamiltonian cycle if and only if $K_{n}$ has a tour of total weight $\leq n$. **Proof of Claim:** - $(\Rightarrow)$ If $G$ has a Hamiltonian cycle, the same cycle in $K_{n}$ uses only edges of weight $1$, giving total weight $n \cdot 1 = n \leq B$. - $(\Leftarrow)$ Suppose $K_{n}$ has a tour of total weight $\leq n$. The tour has exactly $n$ edges. If any edge in the tour has weight $2$, the total weight would be at least $(n-1) \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = n + 1 > n$, contradicting the bound. Therefore, all $n$ edges of the tour must have weight $1$, which means they are all edges of $G$. This tour is a Hamiltonian cycle in $G$. **Polynomial Time:** The construction takes $O(n^{2})$ time to build the weight matrix, which is polynomial. Since Hamiltonian Cycle is NP-Complete, the reduction establishes that TSP-DECISION is NP-Hard. Combined with the fact that a proposed tour can be verified in $O(n)$ time, TSP-DECISION $\in$ NP. Hence, TSP-DECISION is NP-Complete. $\blacksquare$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse **NP-Hard** with **NP-Complete**. Remember: NP-Hard problems need not be in NP (e.g., the Halting Problem is NP-Hard but not in NP). Only $\mathbf{NP} \cap \mathbf{NP\text{-Hard}} = \mathbf{NP\text{-Complete}}$. > - **Common Mistake 2:** When proving NP-Completeness via reduction, students frequently forget to prove that the target problem is *in* NP. The verifier construction is **non-negotiable** and carries significant marks. > - **Common Mistake 3:** In reduction proofs, the direction is **from** the known NP-Complete problem **to** the new problem, not the other way. Writing $L_{new} \leq_{p} L_{old}$ is correct; reversing it invalidates the argument. > - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to verify the **polynomial time bound** of the reduction function. The Examiner specifically checks for $O(n^{k})$ justification. > - **Common Mistake 5:** In numerical subset-sum / knapsack problems, students often forget to show *all* tried combinations or fail to state the certificate explicitly. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** > > - [x] **NP-Complete = NP $\cap$ NP-Hard** — both conditions are mandatory. > - [x] **Cook-Levin Theorem (1971):** SAT is the first NP-Complete problem. > - [x] **Karp's 21 Problems (1972):** The classical reduction catalog. > - [x] **Polynomial-Time Reduction $L_{1} \leq_{p} L_{2}$** preserves YES/NO membership and runs in polynomial time. > - [x] **P $\subseteq$ NP $\subseteq$ PSPACE $\subseteq$ EXPTIME** — strictness of inclusions is open except the first/last. > - [x] **If any NPC $\in$ P, then P = NP** — contrapositive: if P $\neq$ NP, no NPC is in P. > - [x] **Closure Property:** NPC is closed under polynomial-time reductions. > - [x] **Classical NPC Problems to memorize:** SAT, 3-SAT, CLIQUE, INDEPENDENT SET, VERTEX COVER, HAMILTONIAN CYCLE, TSP, GRAPH COLORING, SUBSET SUM, PARTITION, KNAPSACK, SET COVER, DOMINATING SET. > - [x] **Reduction chain to remember:** SAT $\to$ 3-SAT $\to$ (Vertex Cover / Independent Set / Clique / Hamiltonian Cycle) $\to$ TSP. > - [x] **Complement trick:** Independent Set in $G$ $\iff$ Clique in $\bar{G}$. > - [x] **Engineering relevance:** NP-Completeness justifies approximation algorithms, heuristics, randomized algorithms, and quantum algorithms. > - [x] **Cryptographic link:** Many crypto systems (e.g., RSA) rely on the *presumed* hardness of NP-Complete / NP-Hard problems. > - [x] **Verifier requirement:** Every NP problem has a *certificate* of size $O(n^{k})$ verifiable in $O(n^{k})$ time.

topics.title: PSPACE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECST864 - COMPUTATIONAL COMPLEXITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** PSPACE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. PSPACE — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition 1.1 — PSPACE**
> **PSPACE** is the class of all decision problems (languages) that can be solved by a **deterministic Turing machine** which halts after using at most a **polynomial amount of tape space (memory)** on any input of length $n$.
>
> Formally:
>
> $$\text{PSPACE} \;=\; \bigcup_{k \geq 1} \text{DSPACE}\bigl(n^{\,k}\bigr)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition 1.2 — NPSPACE**
> **NPSPACE** is the analogous non-deterministic class: the set of decision problems solvable by a non-deterministic Turing machine using polynomial tape space.
>
> $$\text{NPSPACE} \;=\; \bigcup_{k \geq 1} \text{NSPACE}\bigl(n^{\,k}\bigr)$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Notebook vs Stopwatch" Picture**
> Imagine you are a mathematician trying to verify whether a chess position is a winning one. You don't care *how long* it takes — you may take billions of years — but you are only allowed to use a **finite notebook of pages** (your memory). PSPACE is exactly the set of problems you could solve with that notebook, even if your analysis runs for an astronomical amount of time.
>
> - **Time** = the stopwatch (the clock ticks even when you pause).
> - **Space** = the notebook (the pages you have already written on).
>
> **PSPACE** says: *bound the notebook, not the stopwatch.* Decision problems where the *memory required* grows only polynomially with the input size form PSPACE.

### 1.3 Why PSPACE Matters in Theory

- Many natural combinatorial and game-theoretic problems (e.g., **TQBF**, **Geography**, **Generalized Chess / Go**) are believed to be outside **P** and even **NP**, but are known to be in **PSPACE**.
- It forms the *ceiling* of the polynomial hierarchy: $\text{PH} \subseteq \text{PSPACE}$.
- By **Savitch's Theorem**, **nondeterminism buys nothing extra in space**, so $\text{PSPACE} = \text{NPSPACE}$.

### 1.4 Quick Bookmark of the Standard Chain of Inclusions

$$\text{L} \;\subseteq\; \text{NL} \;\subseteq\; \text{P} \;\subseteq\; \text{NP} \;\subseteq\; \text{PH} \;\subseteq\; \text{PSPACE} \;\subseteq\; \text{EXPTIME}$$

All inclusions in this chain are *believed* to be strict, though only $\text{L} \subsetneq \text{PSPACE}$ and $\text{P} \subsetneq \text{EXPTIME}$ are unconditionally proven (by the Space and Time Hierarchy Theorems).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric for Space-Bounded Classes**
> The unit of "space" on a KTU board exam answer is **tape cells used by a single-tape (or multi-tape) deterministic TM**, excluding the read-only input tape. A polynomial bound means $O(n^k)$ for some fixed $k \geq 1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — What PSPACE Really Captures

- **Step 1 — A decision problem $L$ is in PSPACE if and only if there exists a deterministic TM $M$ and a polynomial $p(n)$ such that for every input $x$ of length $n$, $M$ halts on $x$ with $x \in L$ iff $M$ accepts, and the total number of tape cells ever written is $\leq p(n)$.**
- **Step 2 — The time consumed by $M$ is *not* constrained.** A PSPACE machine may run for $2^{p(n)}$ or even $2^{2^{p(n)}}$ steps. *Why does this not contradict the polynomial-space bound?* Because it can *reuse* cells once the earlier computation is no longer needed. A PSPACE machine on input $x$ of length $n$ is allowed only $O(n^k)$ cells, but it can write and re-write them a super-exponential number of times.
- **Step 3 — PSPACE is closed under complement, polynomial-time reductions, polynomial-space reductions, and (trivially) under union, intersection, and concatenation.** This closure behaviour is what allows us to define **PSPACE-completeness**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Take-Away for the Board Exam**
> Saying "$L \in$ PSPACE" is *not* a claim about runtime. It is a claim about *memory ceiling*. If a question gives you a polynomial-space algorithm, you immediately earn the membership in PSPACE — irrespective of the runtime.

### 2.2 Savitch's Theorem (1970) — The Crown Jewel of Space Complexity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem 2.1 — Savitch's Theorem**
> For any *space-constructible* function $S(n) \geq \log n$,
>
> $$\text{NSPACE}\bigl(S(n)\bigr) \;\subseteq\; \text{DSPACE}\bigl(S(n)^{2}\bigr)$$

**Intuition:** Nondeterminism can be **simulated deterministically** at the cost of *squaring the space* — a small price compared to the exponential blow-up nondeterminism usually costs in time. The proof idea is a *divide-and-conquer reachability* algorithm on the configuration graph.

**Major Corollaries:**

$$\text{NL} \;\subseteq\; \text{DSPACE}(\log^{2} n) \;\subseteq\; \text{P} \qquad \text{(so NL} \subseteq \text{P)}$$

$$\text{PSPACE} \;=\; \text{NPSPACE}$$

### 2.3 PSPACE-Completeness

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition 2.2 — PSPACE-Complete**
> A language $L$ is **PSPACE-hard** if every language in PSPACE polynomial-time reduces to $L$. $L$ is **PSPACE-complete** if, in addition, $L \in \text{PSPACE}$.

**Canonical PSPACE-Complete Problem — TQBF (True Quantified Boolean Formula):**
$$\text{TQBF} \;=\; \{\,\varphi \mid \varphi \text{ is a true fully-quantified Boolean formula}\,\}$$

The proof that TQBF is PSPACE-hard proceeds by simulating an arbitrary polynomial-space-bounded TM via a quantified Boolean formula, with alternating quantifiers encoding the alternation between existential and universal moves.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table collects every formula and boundary condition you may need in a KTU 2024 PSPACE question. Note the use of $\vert$ for the absolute value in the reachability predicate to avoid breaking the markdown table.

| # | Identity / Theorem | Statement | Typical Use |
|---|--------------------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Definition of PSPACE | $\text{PSPACE} = \bigcup_{k \geq 1} \text{DSPACE}(n^{k})$ | Membership proofs |
| 2 | Definition of NPSPACE | $\text{NPSPACE} = \bigcup_{k \geq 1} \text{NSPACE}(n^{k})$ | Recogniser-based proofs |
| 3 | **Savitch's Theorem** | $\text{NSPACE}(S(n)) \subseteq \text{DSPACE}(S(n)^{2})$ for $S(n) \geq \log n$ | Conversion N → D in space |
| 4 | NL $\subseteq$ P corollary | $\text{NL} \subseteq \text{DSPACE}(\log^{2} n) \subseteq \text{P}$ | Proving NL is in P |
| 5 | PSPACE = NPSPACE | By Savitch with $S(n) = n^{k}$ | Closes the N/D gap |
| 6 | Reachability bound | Reachability on $2^{O(S(n))}$ nodes in $O(S(n)^{2})$ space | Proof of Savitch |
| 7 | Ladder of inclusions | $\text{L} \subseteq \text{NL} \subseteq \text{P} \subseteq \text{NP} \subseteq \text{PH} \subseteq \text{PSPACE} \subseteq \text{EXPTIME}$ | Every "place L in hierarchy" question |
| 8 | Space Hierarchy | For space-constructible $S_{1}(n) \in o(S_{2}(n))$, $\text{DSPACE}(S_{1}) \subsetneq \text{DSPACE}(S_{2})$ | Strictness of L $\subsetneq$ PSPACE |
| 9 | PSPACE-complete example | **TQBF**, **Geography**, **Generalized Chess** | Hardness proofs |
| 10 | PH $\subseteq$ PSPACE | $\exists \cdot \forall \cdot \exists \cdots$ quantifier alternations | Relating PH to PSPACE |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of PSPACE Concepts

- **Verification of hardware and software systems (model checking):** Tools like SPIN, NuSMV, and CBMC exhaustively walk through reachable system states. The state space is finite but exponential; by Savitch-style strategies the analysis is run in polynomial *space* even when *time* is exponential.
- **AI game-tree search in two-player zero-sum games with perfect information:** An $n$-ply game tree has $b^{n}$ nodes. A naive DFS only needs depth-$n$ recursion, i.e. polynomial space — this is the formal reason AI engines can play arbitrarily deep games without exploding RAM.
- **Automated theorem proving and SAT-based planning:** The QBF (quantified Boolean formula) solvers used in industry directly attack PSPACE-complete problems, and Savitch-inspired techniques govern their memory footprint.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Proof of Savitch's Theorem (Board-Ready)

**Theorem.** Let $S(n) \geq \log n$ be space-constructible. Then $\text{NSPACE}(S(n)) \subseteq \text{DSPACE}(S(n)^{2})$.

**Construction.** Let $M$ be a non-deterministic TM deciding language $L$ in space $S(n)$. On input $w$ of length $n$, $M$ has at most $c^{\,S(n)}$ distinct configurations for some constant $c$ (each configuration fixes the state, head positions, and tape contents, all bounded by $S(n)$). Acceptance means: starting from the initial configuration $C_{\text{start}}$, $M$ can reach an accepting configuration within $2^{c S(n)}$ steps (because no configuration repeats, otherwise the machine loops).

Define a recursive predicate:
$$\text{REACH}(C_{1}, C_{2}, i) \;\equiv\; \text{configuration } C_{2} \text{ is reachable from } C_{1} \text{ in at most } 2^{i} \text{ moves.}$$

**Goal:** Show that $\text{REACH}(C_{\text{start}}, C_{\text{accept}}, c \cdot S(n))$ can be decided deterministically in $O(S(n)^{2})$ space.

**Recursive evaluation of REACH:**

$$\text{REACH}(C_{1}, C_{2}, i) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
\text{TRUE}, & \text{if } i = 0 \text{ and } C_{1} = C_{2} \text{ or } (C_{1} \vdash C_{2}) \\
\text{TRUE}, & \text{if } i > 0 \text{ and there exists a configuration } C_{\text{mid}} \\
& \quad \text{such that } \text{REACH}(C_{1}, C_{\text{mid}}, i - 1) \\
& \quad \text{and } \text{REACH}(C_{\text{mid}}, C_{2}, i - 1) \\
\text{FALSE}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}$$

**Depth of recursion.** The depth of recursion is the starting $i = c S(n)$. Each recursive call halves $i$. Hence the recursion depth is exactly $c S(n)$.

**Space analysis at each level.** To test the existential "there exists a $C_{\text{mid}}$", we *iterate* deterministically over all $c^{\,S(n)}$ possible mid-configurations. Writing a single configuration takes $O(S(n))$ cells, and at every recursive level we keep on the tape:

1. The current $C_{1}$ and $C_{2}$ (each $O(S(n))$ cells).
2. The current candidate $C_{\text{mid}}$ (another $O(S(n))$ cells).
3. The return value (one bit, negligible).

So each level of the stack uses $O(S(n))$ cells. The maximum depth of the stack is $O(S(n))$. Therefore the total space used is:

$$O\bigl(S(n)\bigr) \;\times\; O\bigl(S(n)\bigr) \;=\; O\bigl(S(n)^{2}\bigr)$$

**Conclusion.** The deterministic procedure accepts $w$ iff $\text{REACH}(C_{\text{start}}, C_{\text{accept}}, c S(n))$ returns TRUE, which holds iff $M$ has an accepting computation. Total deterministic space: $O(S(n)^{2})$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Derivation: NL $\subseteq$ P (Direct Application of Savitch)

We start with the canonical NL-complete problem $\text{PATH} = \{\langle G, s, t \rangle \mid G$ has a directed path from $s$ to $t\}$. The nondeterministic algorithm for PATH uses $O(\log n)$ space to guess the next vertex. Hence $\text{PATH} \in \text{NL}$. Now apply Savitch with $S(n) = \log n$:

$$\text{NL} \;=\; \text{NSPACE}(O(\log n)) \;\subseteq\; \text{DSPACE}\bigl(O(\log n)\bigr)^{2} \;=\; \text{DSPACE}\bigl(O(\log^{2} n)\bigr)$$

A $\text{DSPACE}(\log^{2} n)$ machine runs in time at most

$$2^{O(\log^{2} n)} \;=\; n^{O(\log n)}$$

which is polynomial in $n$. Therefore $\text{DSPACE}(\log^{2} n) \subseteq \text{P}$, and combining:

$$\text{NL} \;\subseteq\; \text{P}$$

This is one of the very few *unconditional* inclusions we can prove between deterministic and nondeterministic classes.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping TQBF to PSPACE

> [!NOTE]
> The reduction is *recursive on the quantifier prefix*. The recursive algorithm for evaluating a fully quantified formula is itself a polynomial-space computation, which is the *reason* TQBF lives in PSPACE.

| Step | Action on Formula $\forall x_1 \exists x_2 \forall x_3 \cdots Q x_k \, \phi$ | Space Used |
|------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------|------------|
| 1 | If $k = 0$, evaluate $\phi$ directly on the empty assignment (linear in $\vert \phi \vert$). | $O(\vert \phi \vert)$ |
| 2 | If outermost is $\forall x_1$, set $x_1 = 0$, recursively evaluate the *remaining* sub-formula; if TRUE, set $x_1 = 1$ and re-evaluate. Return TRUE iff both evaluations return TRUE. | $O(\vert \phi \vert)$ additional |
| 3 | If outermost is $\exists x_1$, similar, returning TRUE if at least one assignment makes the sub-formula TRUE. | $O(\vert \phi \vert)$ additional |
| 4 | Recursion depth = $k \leq \vert \phi \vert$, hence total space = $O(\vert \phi \vert^{2})$? — No, *reuse* the tape on return. The total active tape at any time is $O(\vert \phi \vert)$. | $O(\vert \phi \vert)$ |
| 5 | Conclude TQBF $\in$ PSPACE. Combine with the simulation argument (PSPACE-hardness) for completeness. | — |

The crucial point in Step 4 is that the recursion is **iterative over the quantifier variable's two values**, and the *subroutine* uses the *same* tape region after each call. So the live space remains $O(\vert\phi\vert)$, confirming $\text{TQBF} \in \text{PSPACE}$.

### 3.4 Lower-Bound Argument Sketch: Why TQBF is PSPACE-Hard

Given an arbitrary PSPACE machine $M$ on input $w$ of length $n$, $M$ runs in space $n^{k}$. Construct, in polynomial time, a quantified Boolean formula $\varphi_{M,w}$ such that:

$$M \text{ accepts } w \quad\iff\quad \varphi_{M,w} \in \text{TQBF}.$$

**Construction outline:**
- The configuration graph of $M$ has at most $2^{c n^{k}}$ nodes.
- Reachability in this graph is captured by the predicate $\text{REACH}$ from Savitch's theorem.
- Replace the "$\exists$ mid-configuration" step of REACH by an *existential* quantifier over the bits of $C_{\text{mid}}$.
- Replace the "$\forall$" use of REACH in the deterministic simulation by a *universal* quantifier.
- Recursively expand the reachability check, halving the step budget at each quantifier alternation.
- After $c n^{k}$ alternations, we have a quantified formula of size polynomial in $n$ that is TRUE iff $M$ accepts $w$.

This gives a **polynomial-time many-one reduction** from any PSPACE language to TQBF, proving PSPACE-hardness.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Complexity-Class Ladder Around PSPACE

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef base fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#1F6FEB,color:#0B2545,stroke-width:1px
    classDef mid fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#D97706,color:#5A2A00,stroke-width:1px
    classDef high fill:#E6F8EA,stroke:#1B7F3A,color:#0B3D1A,stroke-width:1px
    classDef outer fill:#F4E8FF,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#2A004A,stroke-width:1px

    nodel1["L"]:::base
    nodel2["NL"]:::base
    nodel3["P"]:::mid
    nodel4["NP"]:::mid
    nodel5["coNP"]:::mid
    nodel6["PH Polynomial Hierarchy"]:::high
    nodel7["PSPACE"]:::high
    nodel8["NPSPACE equal to PSPACE"]:::high
    nodel9["EXPTIME"]:::outer

    nodel1 --> nodel2
    nodel2 --> nodel3
    nodel3 --> nodel4
    nodel3 --> nodel5
    nodel4 --> nodel6
    nodel5 --> nodel6
    nodel6 --> nodel7
    nodel7 <--> nodel8
    nodel7 --> nodel9
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PSPACE Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef in fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#1F6FEB,color:#0B2545
    classDef ctrl fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#D97706,color:#5A2A00
    classDef mem fill:#E6F8EA,stroke:#1B7F3A,color:#0B3D1A
    classDef out fill:#F4E8FF,stroke:#6A1B9A,color:#2A004A

    inputA["Input x length n"]:::in
    ctrlB["Deterministic TM Controller"]:::ctrl
    tapeC["Polynomial Space Tape O of n to the k"]:::mem
    reuseD["Cell Reuse on Recursive Return"]:::mem
    reachE["Recursive REACH Predicate S of n squared"]:::ctrl
    outF["Accept or Reject in PSPACE"]:::out

    inputA --> ctrlB
    ctrlB <--> tapeC
    tapeC --> reuseD
    ctrlB --> reachE
    reachE --> outF
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for "Is $L$ in PSPACE?" (Board-Exam Heuristic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef step fill:#F5F7FA,stroke:#4A5568,color:#1A202C
    classDef yes fill:#C6F6D5,stroke:#22543D,color:#0B3D1A
    classDef no fill:#FED7D7,stroke:#742A2A,color:#3D0B0B
    classDef endp fill:#BEE3F8,stroke:#2A4365,color:#0B2545

    stp1["Step 1 Identify the language L"]:::step
    stp2["Step 2 Construct a TM M for L"]:::step
    stp3["Step 3 Bound the tape cells by p of n"]:::step
    stp4["Step 4 Check whether M reuses cells via recursion"]:::step
    stp5["L is in PSPACE"]:::yes
    stp6["L is in EXPTIME not necessarily PSPACE"]:::no
    stp7["Provide the construction explicitly in the answer"]:::endp

    stp1 --> stp2 --> stp3 --> stp4
    stp4 -- "yes polynomial bound holds" --> stp5
    stp4 -- "no cannot bound by polynomial" --> stp6
    stp5 --> stp7
```
